volume.h1:
SOUTH INDIAN INSCRIPTIONS
VOLUME XXXII
INSCRIPTIONS OF THE EARLY CHOLA KINGS UPTO UTTAMA CHOLA

inscription.head:
No. 1.
(A.R. No. 51 of 1936.)
VĪRACHŌḺAPURAM, TIRUKKOYILUR TALUK,
SOUTH ARCOT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On a hero-stone now preserved in Madras Museum.

p.b: Parakēsarivarmaṉ, who took Tañjai (Vijayālaya) Year 3: C. 853 A.D.

inscription.p:
Records that a certain Karambai Kalituḍaṉ Mukkaṉ of Attiyūr in Kaṟpūṇḍi-
nāḍu died while rescuing cattle from a raid launched by Aṇiyaṉ. The figure of a
warrior aiming an arrow from a bow is carved in relief on the slab.
Published in A.R.Ep., 1935-36. Part II, Page 72. para 34.

inscription.head:
No. 2.
(A.R. No. 266 of 1991.)
EṆṆĀYIRAM, VILLUPURAM TALUK,
VILLUPURAM DISTRICT.
inscription.p:
On the surface of the natural cavern by the side of the Jaina beds in a hilock
known as Mēlkuḍalūr-malai

This inscription may be assigned to the early Chōḷa ruler Vijayālaya, not only
on the grounds of palaeography but also due to the fact that it is found engraved
in continuation of an inscription belonging to the second regnal year of the Pallava
king Nṛipatuṅgavarmaṉ (846-872 A.D.)

It records that an individual Tāḻivaikuntaṉ alias Sembiyaṉ Kāvidiyaraiyaṉ, a
native of Kuḷattūr in Maḻa-nāḍu had gifted five hundred sheep for burning lamps to
the god, (called) Avaṉitilakattu-dēvar at Sēndamaṅgalam in Paṉaiyūr-nāḍu.

inscription.head:
No. 3.
(A.R. No. 69 of 1892.)
ŚRĪRAṄGAM, TIRUCHIRAPPALLI TALUK,
TIRUCHIRAPPALLI DISTRICT.
inscription.p:
Right of entrance into the threshing floor of the fourth prākāra of the
Raṅganāthasvāmin temple.

On account of its archaic characters this record may be assigned to the early
Chōḷa ruler, Rājakēsarivarman Āditya I.

It records that the peruṅguṟi-sabhā of Śrīraṅgam met and resolved that from
the fifth regnal year of the king onwards, those that had abandoned the village (and
had gone elsewhere) would not be eligible to hold the dēvadāna and garden lands
of the temple for management. In violation of this resolution the bhaṇḍāra-vāriyar
and accountant who wrote the lease deeds would be fined 25 poṉ each such
proceeds would be utilized for burning lamps at the temple.
Published in S.I.I. Vol. IV. No. 516.
Ibid Vol. XXIV No, 1.

inscription.head:
No. 4.
(A.R. No. 38 of 1895.)
TILLAISTHĀNAM, THANJAVUR TALUK,
THANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the south wall of the central shrine in the Ghṛitasthnēśvara temple.

This inscription on grounds of palaeography can be assigned to the ninth century
A.D. and hence the Rājakēsarivarman of this record may be identified with
Āditya I.

Records that Ādittaṉ Kaṉṉaradēvaṉ, the son of the Chōḷa king (Sōḻapperumāṉaḍigaḷ)
gifted 20 kaḻañju of gold for burning a perpetual lamp to the god Mahādēvar at
Tirunettānam. The sabhaiyār and the ūrār of the above village received the gold
and undertook to burn the lamp.
Published in S.I.I., Vol. V. No. 595.

inscription.head:
No. 5.
(A.R. No. 12 of 1897.)
SENDALAI, THANJAVUR TALUK,
THANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the west wall of the central shrine in the Sundarēśvara temple.

This inscription on grounds of palaeography can be assigned to the ninth century
A.D. and hence it may be attributed to the period of Āditya I.

It records that Kōyilān Nāganandhi sold his land share Bhārattappaṅgu (land
earmarked for reciting Mahābhāratam), (situated) within the fifty third kūḍumbu
(family-block), to the peruṅguṟi-sabhai of Chandralēkhai-chaturvēdimaṅgalam, a
brahmadēyam in Ārkkāṭṭu-kkūṟṟam. The sabhaiyār in turn assigned this land share
after permanently exempting it from the taxes to Siṅginandil Svarūpa-bhaṭṭan of
Ātreya-gōtra and Vaikhānasa-sūtra, of Perumpuliyūr (Chidambaram) for expounding
Śrībhāratam daily at the ambalam in the brahmasthānam of the village constructed
by Vayiramēgha bhaṭṭaṉ of Veṇṇayil in Miyśeṅgiḷi-nāḍu. The record stipulates that
the person who would inherit and enjoy this land share after him should also recite
Śrībhāratam daily at the brahmasthānam of the village. The sabhā also got the
record engraved on stone.
Published in S.I.I. Vol. VI. No. 12.

inscription.head:
No. 6.
(A.R. No. 286 of 1991.)
GUṆḌŪR, TIRUCHIRAPPALLI TALUK,
TIRUCHIRAPPALLI DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On a stone sluice of the lake of the above village.
This record, on the grounds of palaeography may be assigned to Āditya I.

It records that the great goldsmith (peruṉ-taṭṭāṉ) of (the village) Guṇḍūr, by
name Māṟaṅkuvāvaṉ had got this stone sluice to be constructed.

inscription.head:
No. 7.
(A.R. No. 251 of 1894.)
TIRUVAIYYĀṞU, TANJAVUR TALUK,
TANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p:
On the south wall of the maṇḍapa in front of the central shrine in the
Pañchanandēśvara temple.

This inscription on grounds of palaeography and internal evidence may be
assigned to Āditya I.

It records that Kaḍamba Viḍari, a resident of Kāviri-maṅgalam in Kuṟukkai-
nāḍu, the foster-mother (tādi) of Kaṉṉaradēva donated twenty kaḻañju of gold for
burning a perpetual lamp to the god Mahādēva at Tiruvaiyyāṟu with an uḻakku and
a piḍi of oil by the measure Tiruvaiyyāṟaṉ. With this gold six mā and one kāṇi of
land was obtained after reclamation, for the above purpose.
Published in S.I.I. Vol. V. No. 550.

inscription.head:
No. 8.
(A.R. No. 19 of 1893.)
UKKAL, CHEYYAR TALUK,
NORTH ARCOT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the north wall of the central shrine in the Perumāḷ temple.

It records that the sabhaiyār of Śivachūḷāmaṇi-maṅgalam alias Aparājitach-
chaturvēdimaṅgalam received two hundred kaḷañju of gold from Sēdiyār Tiruvikrama
bhaṭṭar alias Brahmādhirājar, one of the āḷuṅgaṇattārs of Uttaramēruch-
chaturvēdimaṅgalam, a taṉkūṟṟu in Kāliyūr-kōṭṭam and undertook to feed sumptuously.
with the interest of the endowed amount, twelve brāhmaṇas who were well-versed
in vēdas daily at noon, before the God of Bhuvanimāṇikka-Vishṇugriham at
Śivachūḷāmaṇi-maṅgalam was offered food offering. The inscription also enumerates
the dishes to be served to the brāhmaṇas.
Published in S.I.I. Vol. III. No. 1.

inscription.head:
No. 9.
(A.R. No. 5 of 1897.)
TAKKŌLAM, ARKONAM TALUK,
NORTH ARCOT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the south wall of the central shrine in the Jalanāthēśvara temple.

Records that Pridipadiyār (Prithivīpati), son of Māramaraiyar (ie Mārasiṁha)
gifted a silver kettle with a spout weighing three hundred and seventeen kaḻañju to
god of Māhādēvar at Tiruvūṟal, on the occasion of the solar eclipse in the month
of Āṇi.
Published in Ep. Ind. Vol. XIX. pp. 87-88.
Also in S.I.I. Vol. V 1368.

inscription.head:
No. 10.
(A.R. No. 62 of 1936.)
PARAṈŪR, TIRUKKOYILUR TALUK,
SOUTH ARCOT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On a rock below the hill to the north of the village.

This inscription is found clumsily engraved in bold characters. The stone had not
been dressed properly before engraving, hence letters are not clearly visible in
several places. The portion concerned of the rock was ruled before engraving the
inscription.

It records that Ariñjigai Vembaṉ of Paralūr in Uḍaikāṭṭu-nāḍu had caused a
tank (Puttēri) dug and endowed a quarter śey of land for providing a sluice (tūmbu)
to that tank and stipulated that a kuṛuṇi of paddy per mā could be levied for its
maintenance.

inscription.head:
No. 11.
(A.R. No. 214 of 1977.)
SUṆḌAKKĀYMUTTŪR, COIMBATORE TALUK,
COIMBATORE DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On a rock at a place called Pachchapaḷḷi.

p.b: Rājakēsari (Āditya I) In characters of the 9-10th century A.D.

inscription.p:
This inscription is in verse in veṇbā metre and is engraved in vaṭṭeḻuttu
characters, which happened to be popular local script of the region. The verse
eulogises the Chōḷa king as a benevolent one affording shade, shelter and prosperity
to his beloved subjects, as to one whom the prosperous country on the banks of the
Kāvēri belongs, he is also known as Kaṇḍaṉ and Kōḻiyar ko (the lord of the
Kōḻiyar.)
Published in Epigraphia Indica. Vol. XL. P. 18.

For different reading and interpretation of the text see Irāśakēsaripperuvaḻi-
kkalveṭṭu by R. Poongunran in Varalāṟṟuk kalambagam. (1998) pp. 16.

inscription.head:
No. 12.
(A.R. No. 286 of 1911.)
TILLAISTHĀNAM, TANJAVUR TALUK,
TANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the south wall of the central shrine in the Ghritasthanēśvara temple.

This inscription records a gift of 100 sheep for burning a perpetual lamp to the
god at Tiruneyttānam by Kaḍamba Mādēvi, the wife of Vikki Aṇṇaṉ who was the
recipient of several royal honours and of the hereditary title Śembiyaṉ Tamiḻvēḷ
from the Chōḷa king Kōkkaṇḍaṉ alias Rājakēsarivarman, who conquered Toṇḍai-
nāḍu, and the Chēra king Sthāṇu Ravi.
Published in S.I.I. Vol. III No. 89.

inscription.head:
No. 13.
(A.R. No. 226 of 1940.)
TIRUNĀMANALLŪR, TIRUKKOYILUR TALUK,
SOUTH ARCOT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the north wall of the maṇḍapa in front of the Bhaktajanēśvara temple.
This inscription may palaeographically be assigned to Parāntaka I's period.

It records the assignment of twenty-four cheṟuvu of land out of twenty-
five designated as Iriṇīkkī Aḍaiyakoṉaṇkai cheṟuvu for the maintenance of
five servants engaged for sweeping and cleaning the temple premises. The
other remaining one cheṟuvu had been set apart as maintenance for the one
who was blowing the kāḷam.

kappaṭṭa nilam iriṇikki aṭaiyakoṉaṅkaiyāy ceṟuvu
irupattaiñcil loru mākāṇik kāḷaṅkaikku nīkki niṉṟa ceṟuvu
irupattu ṉālum

inscription.head:
No. 14.
(A.R. No. - of 2003.)
OLIYAMAṄGALAM, ILUPPUR TALUK,
PUDUKKOTTAI DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On a stone lying in the tank of the village.

This inscription is in verse composed in kaṭṭaḷaikalittuṟai metre. This record
may be assigned to Parāntaka I not only on account of its paleography but also due
to the occurrence of the name Vīrachōḷa, an epithet assumed by him.

It records that when the peerless Virachōḷa was ruling (a person called)
Chōḷakulanārāyaṇa Mūvēndavēḷ, caused a sluice built to the tank at Ollaiyūr in the
fourth regnal year of the king. He (the builder of the sluice) is eulogized in this
record as generous, he wore a fragrant flower garland and (he was also called)
Kēśavan who belonged to Iḍaikuḍi where Māyavaṉ ie God Vishṇu was pleased to
reside and bless.

inscription.head:
No. 15.
(A.R. No. 84 of 1947-48.)
TIRUPPANDIYŪR, TIRUVALLUR TALUK,
CHENGLEPUT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On a stone-slab lying in the street.
This inscription may be assigned to Parāntaka I on grounds of palaeography.

Records that the ūrār of Paṉṟiyūr in Kāmakkōṭṭappuṟam received gold from
Vimaḍigaḷ son of Paḷḷiyuḍaiyāṉ Tēvaḍi bhaṭṭaṉ of the same village and agreed to
set-apart one kāḍi of paddy per paṭṭi for the maintenance of the big tank of the
village. They undertook the responsibility of carrying out this arrangement, failing
which they agreed to remit one thousand kāṇam daily as fine.

inscription.head:
No. 16.
(A.R. No. 54 of 1948.)
KĀKKALŪR, TIRUVALLUR TALUK,
CHINGLEPUT DISTRICT.
inscription.p:
On a stone-slab with a sculpture of a warrior on its back set up behind
Āñjanēya temple.

This inscription on grounds of palaeography may be assigned to the Chōḷa ruler,
Parāntaka I.

It records that the sabhā made a grant of a paṭṭi of land, freed from taxes, with
its appurtenances (maṉaipaḍappai) and also a share in the tax collected towards
providing protection to the village (pāḍikāval) to the son of Vaḷḷuvan Kaliviriya
Muttaraiyan, a servant of the big men (perumakkaḷ) of the village, Kākkalūr. The
latter (Vaḷḷuvan) bravely defended them when the robbers surrounded (vaḷaitu)
them, and tried to seize their clothes. He fought and drove them. In the course of
fighting he lost his life, and his son, who was also called Kalīvīriya Muttaraiyaṉ, was
given the above compensation.

edition.l: [svasti]śrī [||*] para[k]ecari[ paṉ-
maṟ] ku yāṇṭu āṟāvatu kā-

l.note: In the annual report it is read as kalippaṭṭi.

inscription.head:
No. 17.
(A.R. No. 359 of 1903.)
ANDANALLŪR, TIRUCHIRAPPALLI TALUK,
TIRUCHIRAPPALLI DISTRICT.
inscription.p:
On the south wall of the maṇḍapa in front of the central shrine in the
Vaṭatīrthanātha temple.
The date portion of this record has been read as 19.

Begins with obeisance to Śiva (Namaśivāya). It records that Sembiyan Irukkuvēḷ
alias Bhūtī Parāntakan had got a stone temple constructed for the god Tiruvālanduṟai-
pperumāṉaḍigaḷ at Āndavanallūr in Kiḷiyūr-nāḍu. He then petitioned the king
Parakēsarivarman and obtained three vēli of wet land to be granted in the holdings
of Andavanallūr as kuḍinīkkiya-dēvadānam. At 120 kalam per vēli the paddy per
year to be measured out was 360 kalam of paddy to the temple. However, in the
18th regnal year (925 A.D.) the king Parakēsarivarman sent an order to Sembiyan
Irukkuvēḷ to handover the said land as kuḍinīkkā dēvadāna kāṇikkaḍamai to
Araiyan Vīrachōḷaṉ. Accordingly Sembiyan Irukkuvēḷ instructed his executor, the
tiṇai (revenue official) of Uṟattūr to give effect to it. On this revised basis the paddy
to be measued out was 180 kalam and 120 kalam in the shape of upādi as
determined earlier or in all 310 kalam (But in summing up it is stated that 310 kalam
were to be measured out to the temple. This is not understandable). The customary
water rights were to be enjoyed without obligations of contributing free labour for
the same. (seṉṉīrveṭṭi).
Published on S.I.I. Vol. VIII. No. 668.

inscription.head:
No. 18.
(A.R. No. 231 of 1926.)
KĪḺAPPAḺUVŪR, UDAIYARPALAIYAM TALUK,
TIRUCHIRAPPALLI DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the west wall of the central shrine in the Vaṭamūlēśvara temple.

Records that Nakkaṉ Sāttaṉ, a military official ? (paḍaippēraraiyaṉ) of Paradūr
made a gift of ninety sheep for burning a perpetual lamp with an uḻakku of oil
daily, to the God Tiruvālanduṟai-Mahādēvar at Śiṟupaḻuvūr in Kuṉṟak-kūṟṟam to
propitiate god to secure victory for Kaṇḍaṉ Amudaṉār, the Paḻuvēṭṭaraiyar, in the
battle at Veḷḷūr, where the Pāṇḍya king brought the army of Īḻam and fought
against the king (Perumāṉaḍigaḷ ie Parāntaka).

He made another gift of ten sheep for providing five nāḷi of ghee (to conduct
offerings) on the occasion of Ayaṉasaṅkarānthi.

He further made a gift of 24 sheep for supplying two nāḻi of ghee (to provide
for the bath) every month on the occasion of puṇarpūśam, the natal star of the
above Paḻuvēṭṭaraiyar chief.

The donor further made two gift of four sheep one for anointment of god and
the other for the burning of a lamp on the day of Kārttigai in the Kārttigai month.

inscription.head:
No. 19.
(A.R. No. 1 of 1898.)
UTTIRAMĒRŪR, MADHURANTAKAM TALUK,
CHINGLEPUT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the west wall of the Vaikunthaperumāḷ temple.
For a detailed description on these two inscriptions.
See: V. Venkayya, Two Chōḷa inscriptions at Uttiramallūr

Published in the Annual Report of the Director-General of Archaeology in India
for 1904-1905 pp. 131 ff.

K.A. Nilakanta Sastri, Studies in Chōḷa history and administration (1932),
pp. 163-75.

K.V. Subrahmanya Aiyer, Historical sketches of Ancient Dekhan (1967)
pp. 262 ff.

inscription.head:
No. 19A.
(A.R. No. 2 of 1898.)
UTTIRAMĒRŪR, MADHURANTAKAM TALUK,
CHINGLEPUT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the west wall of the Vaikunthaperumāḷ temple.

inscription.head:
No. 20.
(A.R. No. 167 of 1910.)
ERODE, ERODE TALUK, COIMBATORE DISTRICT.
inscription.p:
On the door jamb of the entrance into the central shrine in
the Raghunāthasvāmi temple.

Records that the resident of Ēḻukarai Tiruvāyppāḍī-nāḍu had agreed to contribute
a voluntary fee for the conduct of the food offerings worship and other ceremonies
to the god, Veṇṇaikkūtta-nāyanār (Kṛishṇa) at the Paḷḷikoṇḍ-āḻvār temple at Erode
on auspicious occasions. The fees specified are as follows:

Half-a-paṇam on each tenant or family (kuḍi) 1/8 paṇam on bride and bridegroom
each and also a kuṉṟi and mañjāḍi as tax towards suḍukāṭṭuppāṭṭam (fees for
cremation). They also undertook to have these arrangements engraved both in stone
and copper-plate.
Published in Indian history and Epigraphy.
Dr. G.S.Gai felicitation volume (1990). p. 75 Plate XI.

inscription.head:
No. 21.
(A.R. No. of 2004-05.)
PAḺAIYAŚĪVARAM, KANCHIPURAM TALUK,
KANCHIPURAM DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the west and south walls of jagati of the ruined Vaikuṇṭa-perumāḷ temple.

Records that the sabhā of Viṇṇapuram in Ūṟṟukkāṭṭu-kōṭṭam met in full
strength at (the hall) Śīrkkuṭṭi-ambalam and passed a resolution relating to the
performance of (the duties of) committees (vāriyam).

It was laid down that only those persons who were amenable both to the sabhā
and kuḍumbu were to perform vāriyam. And while entering to discharge duties (of
vāriyam) they should swear that they would not receive any levies such as ēri-āyam
(levies on account of lake), sōṟumāṭṭu (feeding). āñārasam, brahmasvam except two
kaḻañju of gold per year as expenses of vāriyam (vārpalam). It is also stated that
these (members who were thus selected) should render accounts annually to the
sabhā and declare on both the days of assuming and relinquishing their offices to
that effect. They were also enjoined to do vāriyam only for two years. They
should not do any alteration to the kuḍumbu. It is also laid down that they were
entitled to take food from the villages on those days when they were on (tour from
their native places) to attend (deliberations of the) committee (vāriyam).

inscription.head:
No. 22.
(A.R. No. 226 of 1915.)
BRAHMADĒŚAM, CHEYYAR TALUK, NORTH ARCOT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the south wall of the central shrine in the Chandramauḷīśvara temple.

This inscription records a resolution passed by the aindashṭaśattu-sābhā of
Rājamalla-chaturvēdimaṅgalam to pay to the madhyastha who wrote the accounts
of the tank, four nāḻi of paddy as koṟṟu every day and seven kaḻañju of pure gold
every year and one aṇai (pair) of kūṟai (clothes) per year as his remuneration. It
is also mentioned that in presenting accounts for audit by the assembly, the one who
wrote the accounts would undergo the ordeal of holding the red-hot iron in his hand.
If he came out unscathed and thus was declared pure he would be presented with
a bonus of one-quarter of the surplus (pādasēsham). If he got his hand burnt and
thus failed to establish his purity he would be fined ten kaḻañju of gold but
however he would be exempted from corporal punishment.

inscription.head:
No. 23.
(A.R. No. 252 of 1944.)
VĀLIKAṆḌAPURAM, PERAMBALUR TALUK,
TIRUCHIRAPPALLI DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the south walls of the central shrine in the Vālikaṇḍēśvara temple.

Records that BhUtam Paṇḍāri residing at Maṅgalapuram The Vaigai river bed inscription of Pāṇḍya King Chēndaṉ records the creation of the city Maggalāpuram by
the above King. The editor of the record identified this city with Maggalam in Sattur Taluk Ramanathapuram
District. See (Ep. Ind., Vol. XXXVIII pp. 31-32) It is likely that Maggalāpuram of Vaigai inscription and the one
that occurs in this record are the same.in Pāṇḍi-nāḍu entrusted
seven kaḻañju of gold with the nagarattār for burning a perpetual lamp to the god
Mahādēva of TiruvālIśvaram at VAlikaṇḍapuram in Vaṉ-nāḍu. with the interest of
one uḻakku oil daily by the standard measure of VAlikaṇḍapuram (VAlikaṇḍapurattu
nārāyattāl).

edition.l: maṟku yāṇṭu 18- āva-

inscription.head:
No. 24.
(A.R. No. 176 of 1930.)
PIḶḶAIPPĀKKAM, SRIPERUMBUDUR TALUK,
CHINGLEPUT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On a slab lying in front of the ruined Perumāḷ temple.

This inscription along with the other one found at Tiruniṉṟavūr, a village in the
above taluk and district, throw welcome light on the local administration of
(Tiru)niṉṟavūr during the time of Parāntaka I.

This highly damaged inscription is in two pieces. At the commencement of the
tenth line onwards, a few letters are missing consistently as the slab is broken at the
proper left.

It registers a regulation made by the assembly consisting of the great men
(perumakkaḷ) of the sabhā of Niṉṟavūr in Palkuṉṟak-kōṭṭam, the great men
representing the wards (kuḍumbināl maṉṟāḍugira perumakkaḷ) of the village and a
certain officer named Pakūr-Tiruvaḍi, probably representing the crown. The clauses
of this regulation which are pieced together from the extant portion of the record
may be summed up as follows:

1. With effect from the 20th regnal year (927 A.D.) two persons (pēr) who
had no previous experience (ariyādār) as members of the assembly shall
represent each ward. (kuḍumbil).

2. That the taxes on lands shall thence-forward be paid through the wards
(kuḍumbin vaḻīyē iṟai iṟuppadāgavum).

3. It is also stated here that the lands on which taxes had not been paid shall
be sold and the amount due on them realized.

4. That persons acting against the stipulated conditions of this resolution
shall be liable to a daily fine one mañjāḍi of gold and the fine so
imposed shall be duly collected.

5. The remuneration for the services rendered by the madhyastha shall be
given at the rate of one kaḻañju of gold per month and cloth (kūṟai)
every six months.

6. That the two of the great men (perumakkaḷ) and one another individual
(oruvanukku) shall be paid half kaḻañju per month and a cloth every six
months (as in clause 5).

Published in Indian History and Epigraphy
Dr. G.S. Gai felicitation volume (1990) pp. 80-81.

edition.l: [madhya] staṉu[k] ke • ka

inscription.head:
No. 25.
(A.R. No. 476 of 1925.)
PAḶḶIKOṆḌAI, VELLORE TALUK, NORTH ARCOT DISTRICT.
inscription.p:
On the left door jamb of the maṇḍapa in front of the central shrine in the
Nāganāthēsvara temple.
Records that Karuvili Muttaraiyaṉ had caused the aradha-maṇḍapa to be built.

inscription.head:
No. 26.
(A.R. No. 444 of 1904.)
VĒDĀRAṆYAM, TIRUTTURAIPPUNDI TALUK, TANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p:
On the third pillar in the maṇḍapa of the central shrine in the
Vēdāraṇyēśvara temple.

It records the gift of 60 kaḻañju of gold to god Mahādēva of Tirumaṟaikkāḍu,
a perundēvadāṉa in Umbaḷa-nāḍu, for the maintenance (jīvitam) of one brāhmaṇa
well versed in the vēdas and archchanāvidhi serving the god during the 3 sandhis,
by Vasan Dē[var]. Kaḍambaṉ of Māṉanilainallūr, a brahmadēya in Kaḷakkuḍi-nāḍu
in Pāṇḍi-nāḍu. The sabhā of Naṟukaḍambīḍu-chchaturvēdimaṅgalam, a brahmadēya
in the same nāḍu, received the amount and agreed to provide paddy for the purpose,
as interest, on the amount endowed.
Published in S.I.I. Vol. XVII. No. 475.

inscription.head:
No. 27.
(A.R. No. 195 of 1994-95.)
OḺUGAIYŪR, WALAJAPET TALUK, NORTH ARCOT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On a stone slab set up in the lake of the village.

Damaged at the end. Records that Sembiyan Māvalivāṇarāyaṉ had caused a
sluice made to the lake at Oḻugaiyūr in Oḻugai-nāḍu (a sub-division) of Paḍuvūr-
kōṭṭam, after his name as Pṛithivīpati-pperumtūmbu. The nāṭṭār of Oḻugaiyil-nāḍu
undertook to contribute a kuṭṭai of paddy per paṭṭi obviously for the maintenance
of the sluice for the merit of Sembiyaṉ Māvalivāṇarāyar.

Published in the Quarterly journal of the Mythic Society.
Vol. XCI (2000) p. 69.

inscription.head:
No. 28.
TIRUNIṈṞAVŪR, SRIPERUMBUDUR TALUK,
CHINGLEPUT DISTRICT.
inscription.p:
On two pieces of stone, one a stone pillar fixed into the ceiling of the
circumambulatory maṇḍapa (western side) of the Āṇḍāḷ shrine in the Bhaktavatsala-
perumāḷ temple and another, a loose stone lying near the temple's car.

These two stone fragments (found at Tiruniṉṟavūr) contain writing in characters
of about the tenth century A.D. They are apparently parts of a single inscription.
The contents of these inscriptions, the palaeography of writing and the high regnal
year (23) as cited suggest that just as the Uttiramērūr and the other Piḷḷaippākkam
inscription, this inscription also belongs to the reign period of Parāntaka chōḷa I.

It refers to the assembly of the mahāsabhā including maṉṟāḍugiṉṟa-sabhā,
ērivāriyapperumakkaḷ (tank committee). tōṭṭavāriyapperumakkaḷ (greatmen of the
garden comittees). bhaṭṭapperumakkaḷ (priests), visishṭapperumakkaḷ (body of eminent
people) met in the pērambalam (big hall) without the distinction, of the youth or
elders, in position and functioning from the kumbha month in the regnal year
23 (of the reign of the king) and the passing of the resolution for the welfare of
the village. Accordingly for electing members for the task of writing the minutes
of the proceedings and the resolutions passed in the meetings of the maṉṟāḍus and
vāriyams, the ballot-chits would be prepared after ensuring that the aspirants satisfied
the following norms. (Or the resolution passed by them was to the following effect:)
while conducting the elections to the maṉṟāḍu (advisory committee) and vāriyams
it should be ensured that the names of only those who fulfilled the following
conditions were entered in the chits. (ballot papers).
1. Those persons were congenial to the sabhā.

2. Prior to that date they had not been the members of the maṉṟāḍus and
vāriyams during the preceding five years.

3. Their relatives ie uterine brothers and sisters, their fathers and their
children had not functioned in the same vāriyam and maṉṟāḍu in the
preceding two years.

Those that were thus selected would be presented in the mahāsabhā in its open
session. Those retiring from the duties in the maṉṟāḍu and vāriyam committees
would show to the sabhā the accounts for the period they were functioning in the
said comittees and (satisfy them). Those transgressing the above arrangement would
pay a fine of 25 kaḻañju of gold daily in the dharmāsanam (court of justice).
Published in Āvaṇam No. 7 (1996) pp. 165-167)

edition.l: brahmasthānatte ku Read kūṭiyiruntu-
edition.p:
On another stone lying near the chariot, probably in
continuation of the above inscription.

inscription.head:
No. 29.
(A.R. No. 360 of 1903.)
ANDANALLŪR, TIRUCHIRAPPALLI TALUK,
TIRUCHIRAPPALLI DISTRICT.
inscription.p:
On the south wall of the maṇḍapa in front of the central shrine in the
Vatatīrthanātha temple.

Records that three vēli of dēvadāna land at Andavanallūr, which Araiyaṉ
Vīrachōḷaṉ obtained (as tenant-cultivator) gave it up in favour of the ūrār of
Andavanallūr.

Note: The individual Araiyaṉ Vīrachōḻan obtained the right to cultivate the land
as kuḍinīkka (unextinguishable tenant) from the king himself and the land dues
assigned to the temple was reduced to 310 kalam from 360 kalam. He in his turn,
had conveyed his rights of inextinguishable tenancy to the ūrār.
Published in S.I.I. Vol. VIII No. 669.

l.ta:
rt tevatāṉam muveli ṉilamum nāṉ peṟṟuṭaiya paricey aṉtava
nallūr ru-

[rā][r*]kku viṭṭuk kuṭutteṉ aṉtavanallū [ru]rārkku araiyaṉ
viracoḻaṉeṉ [|*] itu

inscription.head:
No. 30.
(A.R. No. of 2004-05.)
SĪYAMAṄGALAM, WANDIWASH TALUK,
TIRUVANNAMALAI DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On a pillar in the mahamaṇḍapa of the cave temple.

Records that Porimaiyaṉ son of Koṇḍi Araiyar, the brother-in-law of Seya
Mukkaraiyar (Seya Muttaraiyar ?) had raised it probably (mahāmaṇḍapa).

inscription.head:
No. 31.
(A.R. No. 335 of 1902.)
TIRUNĀMANALLŪR, TIRUKKOYILUR TALUK,
SOUTH ARCOT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the south wall of central shrine in the Bhaktajanēśvara temple.

This inscription records a gift of one Īḻa lamp and ninty sheep for burning a
perpetual lamp to the god of Tiruttoṇḍīśvaram, at Tirunāvalūr by Chittirakōmaḷam,
a maid-servant of the queen Kōkkiḻānaḍīgaḷ, the mother of Rājādittadēvar who had
caused the temple built in stone.
Published in Ep. Ind., Vol. VII pp. 133-134

inscription.head:
No. 32.
(A.R. No. 384 of 1913.)
EṞUMBŪR, CHIDAMBARAM TALUK, SOUTH ARCOT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the south wall of the central shrine, Kadambavanēśvara temple.

This inscription records the raising of the śrī-vimānam (central shrine) in stone
and the construction of the eight attendant gōpurams for the god (Perumānaḍigaḷ)
of the small temple (chiṟu-ttirukkōyil) at Urumūr, a dēvadānam in Nalvayalūr-
kkūṟṟam on the northern bank (of the river Koḷḷiḍam ?) by Iruṅkōḷaṉ Guṇavaṉ
Aparājitaṉ. The latter also endowed three and a quarter vēli of land duly freed
from taxes as the capital for maintaining the services in that temple (uṇṇāḻigaippuṟam),
after duly supplicating the overlord (perumāṉaḍigaḷ) Chōḷa Parāntaka. The three
and a quarter vēli of land was composed of three vēli, in the village Kārai
Vāḻkkaiyilāṉpāḍi and one quarter chey (vēli) in the village Charukkai. The
inscription also records that besides this, the donor made a grant of another three
and quarter vēli of land apparently freed from taxes, in two plots with the
customary water rights, in two villages. Out of this, three vēli of land was in Āḷpāḍi
and a quarter vēli of land in Dugaiyāḷ. They were earmarked for the following
purposes.

1. One and a quarter vēli of land for meeting the expenses of the entire
worship (tiruchchennaḍaippuṟam).

2. One chey (one vēli) for the livelihood of the worshipping priests
(archaṉābhōgam).
3. One chey for the maintenance of the drummers (tiruppalipuṟam).

l.ta:

inscription.head:
No. 33.
(A.R. No. 739 of 1905.)
GRĀMAM, TIRUKKOYILUR TALUK, SOUTH ARCOT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the west wall of the central shrine in the Śivalōkanātha temple.

Records a gift of 100 sheep for burning a perpetual lamp with an uḻakku of oil
daily for God Śrī Āṟṟuttaḷi Śrī Mūlasthānattu-Mahādēvar in Tirumuḍiyūr by
Veḷḷaṅkumaraṉ, a native of Nandikaraiputtur in Malaināḍu, the commander of the
big army of the prince Rājāditta.

l.note: A letter is found engraved between nū and ṟu and it appears to have been erased.

inscription.head:
No. 34.
(A.R. No. 1 of 1896.)
KĪḺ-MUṬṬUGŪR, GUDIYATTAM TALUK, NORTH ARCOT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On a stone slab set up in a field.

It records that when cattle were lifted at Mukkuṭṭūr by Perumānaḍigaḷ, Vadunavāraṉ
Varadaṉ Tāṇḍaṉ recovered the cattle but died.
Published in Ep. Ind., Vol. IV. p. 179.

edition.l: ṉ vatu[ na-
v] āraṉ-

inscription.head:
No. 35.
(A.R. No. 180 of 1921.)
PERIYAVĀRIKKAM, GUDIYATTAM TALUK, NORTH ARCOT
DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On a slab fixed in the tank weir.

Records the sabhā of Vārikkayam alias Chandrāditya-maṅgalam granted five
hundred kuḻi of land as nettal paṭṭi to the wife and children of Sāmaṉāyakan Sāttaṉ
Tāḻi, the bodyguard (aṇukkili) of the prince Rājakēsari-panma bhaṭṭakēsari, who died
in the skirmish during a raid for seizing buffaloes at the above place.

Published in Quarterly Journal of Mythic Society.
LXXVI (1985) pp. 149-150.

inscription.head:
No. 36.
(A.R. No. 186 of 1921.)
KUMĀRAMAṄGALAM, GUDIYATTAM TALUK,
NORTH ARCOT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On a hero-stone set up in the west of the village.

It records the death of Maṉukulaṉ Siṅgaperumāṉ, son of Iḷakāmuṇḍaṉ of
Mēlaḍaiyūr-nāḍu, in a cattle-raid after the recovery of the buffloes.

inscription.head:
No. 37.
(A.R. No. 91 of 1995-96.)
TIRUVĒNDIPURAM, CUDDALORE TALUK,
SOUTH ARCOT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On a pillar in the prākāra of the Dēvanāyagapperumāḷ temple.

Damaged at the end. This inscription records a gift of ninety sheep for burning
a perpetual lamp to the god of Tiruveyintirapuram by Vāṉavaṉmādēviyār, the
daughter of the Pāṇḍyaṉ ruler, Rāsasiṅgaperumāṉār (Rājasiṁha).
Published in Āvaṇam., No. 7 (1996) p. 21.

inscription.head:
No. 38.
VEṆMANDAI, WANDIWASH TALUK,
NORTH ARCOT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On a rock in the tank of the village.

Records that Kaśuvaraṉ Tiruppaḻaṉaśeydāṉ, the Kiḻavaṉ (head man) and the
leading citizen (talaimagaṉ) of Karuvūr who was given the charge of the headmanship
of Karuvūr in Veṅgala-nāḍu took over the ērippaṭṭi (land granted for the maintenance
of the reservoir) at Veṇpaḻandai in Vādavūr-nāḍu (a sub-division) of Veṇkuṉṟa-
kōṭṭam after it had been measured and had the sluice for the lake constructed and
set apart the rest of the land as ērippaṭṭi.
Published in Āvaṇam., Issue No. 7 July 1996 p. 161.

inscription.head:
No. 39.
(A.R. No. 63 of 1905.)
ĀṈAIMALAI, MADURAI TALUK,
MADURAI DISTRICT.
inscription.p:
On a rock at the entrance into the central shrine in the Narasiṅgapperumāḷ
temple.

p.b: M.K. Parakēsarivarman (Parāntaka I) Year 33 Aslēsha, Kaṟkaṭaka, Friday, 939 A.D. July 19.

inscription.p:
This inscription is in vaṭṭeḻutu characters. It records an agreement given by the
members of the assembly of Narasiṅga-maṅgalam, a brahmadēya and a dēvadāna
included in the Kiḻ-Iraṇiyamuṭṭam, to Marudūr-uḍaiyāṉ Aruṇidi Kaliyaṉ of Marudūr
in Puṟaṅkaṟambai-nāḍu, an official of the Chōḷa king. The temple of
Narasiṅgapperumāṉaḍigaḷ of Tiruvāṉaimalai had to remit 18 īḻakkāśu to the sabhā
on account of taxes on its land. To realize a part of this amount, six īḻakkāśu,
fifteen īḻakkāśu had been deposited by the official with the sabhaiyār. To meet the
balance of twelve īḻakkāśu, the above official (again) deposited tax-money
(Iṟaikkāval) and obtained the possession of the land (with a tank therein) called
Ūruḍaiyāṉkuḷam alias Kaliyaṉēri with the right to utilize the water. He then
purchased two vēli of land adjacent to the tank. The produce 300 kalam of paddy
from this (plot of) land were to be utilized, half ie 150 kalam for providing food
offerings to the god thrice a day and the other half to feed five brāhmaṇas well-
versed in vēdas, commencing from the day of solar eclipse in the month of
Kaṟkaṭaka on Friday when the star Aslēsha was current. It is mentioned that the
śrīkāriyam (the temple official), Tiruvāyppāḍi-Nārāyaṇaṉ and the members of the
above assembly gave an undertaking to conduct this feeding ceremony without fail.
They also agreed to remit a fine of two kāśu daily if they failed to carry out this
charity. Other details relating to the fines are given.
Published in S.I.I. Vol. III No. 106.

l.ta:

inscription.head:
No. 40.
(A.R. No. 230 of 1903.)
TOṆḌAIMĀNĀḌ, CHITTOOR TALUK,
CHITTOOR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On a base of the central shrine in the Kōdaṇḍaramēśvara temple.

Records that the sabhā and the nagarattār of Toṇḍaimāṉpērāṟṟūr in Āṟṟūr-nāḍu
in Tiruvēṅkaṭa-kōṭṭam received one hundred and five kaḻañju of gold duly tested
for purity as also four thousand kāḍi of paddy (by the measure Vāgīśvara-paṇḍittaṉ)
from the pontiff paḷḷippaḍai Vāgīśvara-paṇḍita bhaṭārar for conducting seven day
festival commencing on the day of asterism of kēṭṭai and ending with sadaiyam in
the puraṭṭādi month at the temple Śrī Kōdaṇḍarāmēśvaram alias Ādityēśvaragrihm
as well as for providing food offerings on that occasion. The sabhaiyār and the
nagarattār were to apportion the interest of one thousand kāḍi of pady accrued
annually out of the endowed four-thousand kāḍi for feeding the mendicants,
brāhmaṇas and devotees on those seven days at the temple of various faiths.
Reference is also made to the festival, Indra-viḻa. The manner in which these
benefaction were intended to be carried out is laid out in detail. It was also
enjoined on the mahāvṛattins of the temple Ādityēśvaram, the pṛithiviṭaṅkak-
gaṇapperumakkaḷ of Tiruppaṉṟīśvaram at Kāvirippākkam and panmāhēśvarak-kaṇkāṇi
were to oversee the prompt supply of paddy and proper execution of the charity by
the two bodies, the sabhaiyār and the nagarattār. It is interesting to note that if
these two bodies defaulted in the carrying out of these benefactions as detailed
above, they could be tortured and coerced by the above three bodies and got them
to carry out the benefaction for which they had given an undertaking.
Published in S.I.I. Vol. VIII. No. 529.

inscription.head:
No. 41.
(A.R. No. 46 of 1923.)
PUḶḶALŪR, KANCHIPURAM TALUK,
CHINGLEPUT DISTRICT.
inscription.p:
On stone built indifferently into the walls of the Kāmākshi Amman shrine in the
Kailāśanātha temple.

Records that the queen Seyya(Jaya ?)bhuvanasundaramaṇiyār donated a lamp-
stand as well as ten kaḻañju of gold for burning a perpetual lamp, to the god Śrī
Rāghavar, who is pleased to stand at Tiruvayōdhi, at Pulvēḷūr in Eyiṟ-kōṭṭam with
the interest of one uḻakku of oil daily. The Gaṇapperumakkaḷ undertook to have the
lamp burnt.

It is also recorded that the sabhaiyār of Pulvēḷūr took over the gold entrusted
to the gaṇapperumakkaḷ by the queen and undertook the responsibility of burning
the lamp perpetually. The sabhaiyār also agreed to pay a fine of eight and half-a-
kāṇam daily by the officials of dhanmāśanam if they defaulted.

l.note: The Letter sti is engraved below the line.
l.ta:
śrī [||*] matiraikoṇṭa kopparakecaripaṉmaṟku yāṇṭu muppattu
nālāvatu ivvāṇṭu nampirāṭṭiyār ceyya

The Letter ḷa is engraved below the line.
The Letter cai is engraved below the line.
The Letter ta is engraved below the line.
The Letter m is engraved below the line.

There is an unnecessary stroke over the letter na like ni and the long vowel it is engraved
like 7 which however may be iṅnored and read vilakkinār •

kāṇa[m*] taṇṭamiṭuvat[ā*]ka oṭṭik ku[ṭu*]ttom pulveḷur

inscription.head:
No. 42.
(A.R. No. 96 of 1892.)
UYYAKKOṆḌĀṈ-TIRUMALAI, TIRUCHIRAPPALLI TALUK,
TIRUCHIRAPPALLI DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On a pillar in the verandah of the Ujjivanātha temple.

Records that Pirāntakaṉ Madēvaḍigaḷ, the wife of Pirāntaka Gaṇḍarādittadēvar
and daughter of Maḷapperumāḷ, made a gift of ninety sheep for burning a perpetual
lamp with one uḻakku of oil to be supplied daily by the measure śūla-uḻakku to the
god Paramēśvara of Tirukaṟkuḍi in Nandipanmamaṅgalam, a brahmadēyam on the
south bank (of the river Kāvēri).
Pulished in S.I.I. Vol, IV No. 543.

inscription.head:
No. 43.
(A.R. No. 68 of 1890.)
SŌMŪR, KARUR TALUK, TIRUCHIRAPPALLI DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the south wall of the Sōmēśvara temple.

Records the donation of the paḍai (lower stratum) of the temple vimāna by
Mummaḍikkīṟaṉ Irāmi of Kēraḷapaḷḷi.
Published in S.I.I. Vol, IV No. 392.

inscription.head:
No. 44.
(A.R. No. 735 of 1905.)
GRĀMAM, TIRUKKOYILUR TALUK, SOUTH ARCOT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the north wall of the central shrine in the Śivayōganātha temple.

p.b: M.K. Parakēsarivarman (Parāntaka I) Year 36: Kali 4044, 1477037 day, Makara, Sunday, star Rēvati = 943 A.D. 14th January

inscription.p: This inscription is bilingual ie Sanskrit and Tamil.

The Sanskrit portion records that Veḷḷaṅkumara, a native of Puttūr, was bold
and invincible in battles and he was the best of the Kēraḷites. He was a general of
(Prince) Rājāditya and built a stone temple for the god Mahādēva at the village
Mauligrāma, situated on the bank (of the river) Peṇṇai.

The Tamil portion repeats the some fact. It records that Veḷḷaṅkumaraṉ, a native
of Nandikkaraiputtur in Maḷai-nāḍu was a chief servant of the Chōḷas and was a
great general of Rājāditya, the son of Parāntaka alias Vīrasōḻa perumāṉaḍigaḷ. It
further mentions that he consecrated (an image of) Āṟṟuttaḷi-perumāṉaḍigaḷ which
was sixteen feet in height, inside the stone temple, with libation of water.
Published in A.S.I. Annual Report 1905-06, pp. 182-184.

edition.l: ttu nālu matiraikoṉṭa Read matiraikoṇṭa kopparakecaripaṉma[k] Read paṉmaṟku-

l.note: Restored as eḻupatteḻāyirattu (see Ep. īnd. Vol. VIII P. 261.)
This was wrongly read as kumbha dāraiyāl and corrected into kumbha dhāraiyāl

inscription.head:
No. 45.
(A.R. No. 99 of 1929.)
LALGUDI, LALGUDI TALUK, TIRUCHIRAPPALLI DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the south wall of the central shrine in the Saptarīshīśvara temple.

Records the endowment of thirty kaḻañju of gold for the purpose of paying the
remuneration of 2 brāhmaṇas reciting tiruppadiyam on all the three twilight periods
daily by Śivakōsari Piḍāraṉ Dēśaviṭaṅkaṉ, a priest of temple (ittaḷippaṭṭuḍaiyāṉ) of
Mahādēva of Tiruttavattuṟai, a dēvadāna village in Iḍaiyāṟṟu-nāḍu. Against this gold
Kiḍāmbil Ichchuvara Naḍuppaya kramavittaṉ of Kuṇdina-gōtrā (Kauṇdinya-gōtra) of
the dēvadāna-brahmadēya village, Anuttiramaṅgalam sold (set apart) two mā of his
share-land in two plots (to be retained and administered by him) and agreed to
contribute thirty nāḻi of oil per year for the stated purpose.

l.note:
The first two letters in the word pitrukkaḷ are found engraved like pṛ which tempts one to read
as gurukkaḷ

l.ta: kanukku nāṉ viṟṟa nilattukku kiḻakkayyaṭi akalam teṉ
maṭal oḻu[k]kaikkuk kiḻakkum kāviri niroṭu kālukku teṟ-
kkum ivvicaitta perunāṉkellaiyil akappaṭṭa nila muṇ-
ṟṟu iññilattāl nānaṭ[ṭa]k kaṭava eṇṇai āṇṭu varai patiṉ
koṇṭu iññilam vilaikkaṟa viṟṟu kuṭu[t*]teṉ cuvara naṭuppaya

edition.l: 30- yum ippiṭāraṉ tecaviṭaṅkaneṉ nāṉ iccuvaranaṭuppaya kra-

l.ta: mavittaniṭai nāṉ vilaikoṇṭa mekkaṭaiyaṉta pāti nilattiluma-

yu māṇṭu varai nāṉ koṇṭa
ṉilattile tirumāvukku
l.note:
It is evident that the engraver in the first instance had forgotten to incise this line but later on
to accommodate the line he inserted it in small letters, after the whole matter was engraved.

inscription.head:
No. 46.
(A.R. No. 143 of 1925.)
TIRUVĀḌUTUṞAI, MAYAVARAM TALUK, TANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the east wall of the central shrine in the Gōmuktīśvara temple.

Records that when the King worshipped the deity at Tiruvāvaḍutuṟai in Śāttaṉūr,
a brahmadēyam in Tiraimūr-nāḍu, on seeing that the shrine was being constructed
in stone he donated five hundred poṉ to cover the shrine from Kuḍappaḍai
upwards, in gold.

edition.l: rivaṟma[ṟ]kku yāṇṭu 38 āvatu [ kaṉṉi nā-
yaṟṟu] tiraimurnāṭṭu brahmateyam cāttaṉūr

inscription.head:
No. 47.
(A.R. No. 430 of 1922.)
ĀNANDAMAṄGALAM, MADHURANTAKAM TALUK,
CHINGLEPUT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On a rock near the Jaina image.

This inscription is clumsily engraved in bold characters. It records a gift of five
kaḻañju of gold for feeding perpetually one devotee at the monastery Jinagirippaḷḷi
by Varddhamāṉap-periyaḍigaḷ, a disciple of Viṉaiyābhasūrakkuravaḍigaḷ, the pontiff
of monastery (paḷḷi) at Śēvūr. It also records the setting up of (an image) Paḷḷiyāṉ
by Śāntiśēnakkuravaḍiyaḷ.

Another interperation is that it registers a gift of five kaḻañju of gold by
Śāntiśēnakkuravaḍiyaḷ for feeding perpetually one devotee at the monastery, Jinagirippaḷḷi
by Varddhamāṉap-periyaḍigaḷ, a disciple of Viṉaiyābhasūrakkuravaḍigaḷ, the pontiff
of monastery at Śēvūr to whom the gold endowed was entrusted.

inscription.head:
No. 48.
(A.R. No. 220 of 1937.)
KARAḌI, TIRUKKOYILUR TALUK, SOUTH ARCOT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: South side of a boulder near the ruined Śiva temple in the fort.

Records a gift of ninty-six sheep by Vīranāraṇiyār, the wife of the prince
Gaṇḍarādittar, the son of the queen, Chōḷamādēviyār, for burning a perpetual lamp
to the god Tirumāḍappāṟai-Mahādēvar at Ravikulaśūḷāmaṇi-chaturvēdimaṅgalam (situated
on the) south (bank) of the (river) Peṇṇai with an uḻakku of oil daily. The gift was
entrusted to the sabhaiyār of Ravikulaśūḷāmaṇi-chaturvēdimaṅgalam. It is also stated
that this charity was put under the supervision of Puduvūr-uḍaiyāṉ.

l.ta: vaitta cāvāmuvā perāṭu toṇ[ṇū]ṟṟāṟu itu paṉmā-

l.note: There seems to be a letter above the letter i in a smaller form, which resembles the letter ra

inscription.head:
No. 49.
(A.R. No. 82 of 1896.)
SUCHĪNDIRAM, KANYAKUMARI TALUK,
KANYAKUMARI DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On a boulder in the Sthāṇunātha temple.

p.b: M.K. Parakēsarivarman (Parāntaka I) Year 40: Kumbha, (Jan-Feb): 947 A.D.

inscription.p:
Records that Oruṁgai Araṅgaṉ, a merchant of Karavandapuram in Kaḷakkuḍi-
nāḍu donated a lamp-stand weighing thirty palam and seventy-five sheep for burning
a perpetual lamp to the god of Tiruchchivindiram with one and half uḻakku of ghee
daily. Further he donated twenty-five sheep for burning another perpetual lamp at
Tiruvēṅkaṭanilai with one āḻākku of ghee daily. These 100 sheep (75+25) were
entrusted to the paraḍaichchavaiyār (parishad-sabhā-temple assembly).
Published in E.P. Ind., vol. V p. 43.

inscription.head:
No. 50.
(A.R. No. 156 of 1969.)
TIRUVOṞṞIYŪR, SAIDAPET TALUK,
CHINGLEPUT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On a pillar stone supporting Nandi, Ādhipurīśvara temple (west face).

It records that Nakkaṉ Parāntakaṉ alias Brahmaśrīrājaṉ (a resident) of Alaiyūr
a brahmadēyam in Mipilāṟu in Poygai-nāḍu on the northern bank (of the river
Kāvēri) in Chōḷa-nāḍu made a gift of ninety sheep for burning a perpetual lamp to
the god of Tiruvoṟṟiyūr.

l.note: The letter ya is engraved below.

inscription.head:
No. 51.
(A.R. No. 386 of 1924.)
MĒLAPPAḺAUVŪR, UDAIYARPALAIYAM TALUK,
TIRUCHIRAPPALLI DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the north wall of the Choḷēśvara shrine in the Āgastyēśvara temple.

This inscription records an undertaking given by the seven officials of the
temple of Avanigandharva-Īśvaragṛiham to Nakkaṉ Aiyyāṟṟaḍigaḷ, a dancing-girl of
the temple. She purchased four mā of waste land at Uṟagaṉkuḍi, a dēvadāna village
of Poygai-nāḍu. She reclaimed it converted it into wet-land and donated back to the
temple for burning a perpetual lamp out of the produce of the land.

l.ta: ṭu kalli iraṇṭu pūvum viḷaiya nirnilam nāṉku māvum
ṉtā viḷakku iravum pakalum erippomānom ittaḷi-

inscription.head:
No. 52.
(A.R. No. 149 of 1937.)
KUMĀRAVAYALŪR, LALGUDI TALUK,
TIRUCHIRAPPALLI DISTRICT.
inscription.p:
On the south wall of the maṇḍapa infront of the central shrine in the
Agnīśvara temple.

It records that Nālāyirattu Muṉṉūṟṟuvaṉ alias Chandraśēkharaṉ Aramaiyindaṇ,
the madhyasthan (the arbitrator) of Nandipanma-maṅgalam, a brahmadēyam on the
southern bank (by the river Kāvēri) had dedicated his maid servant, whom he had
acquired in the 35th year (i.e. 942 A.D. of the ruling king) Ūraṉ Śōlai, her daughter
Veḷḷāḷa Pirāṭṭi and the latter's daughter Aramaiyindaṉ Kaṇḍi with the libation of
water to the god, Paramēśvarar of the stone temple at Vayalūr for serving as chauri-
bearers as well as for reciting tiruppadiyam.

l.ta:
eṉ aṭiyāḷ ūraṉ colaiyaiyyum ivaḷ makaḷ
veḷḷāḷapirāṭṭiyaiyum ivaḷ makaḷ aramaiyiṉtaṉ kaṇṭiy[ai]yum
uḷḷiṭṭārai u ••••

ra[r]kku kavarippiṇāvākavum
tiruppatiyam pāṭavum ni(r)roṭu aṭṭik kuṭutteṉ cantiracekaraṉ
aramaiyiṉtaṉe[ṉ*]

inscription.head:
No. 53.
(A.R. No. 345 of 1903.)
NAṄGĀVARAM, KULITTALAI TALUK,
TIRUCHIRAPPALLI DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the base of the central shrine in the Piḍāri temple.

Records that 12(1/2) kalam of paddy per annum accrued by way of interest at the
rate of two nāḻi per kalam per month, on the fifty kalam of paddy gifted by an
individual named •••• teḍakki ulagaṉ (apparently for providing offerings and
worship) to the goddess Araṇaḻippa Mākāḷattukkāḷa-bhaṭāri alias Sāttaṉūr Naṅgai for
the prevalence of peace, prosperity and well-being of Ariñjigaich-chaturvēdimaṅgalam,
a dēvadāna-brahmadēya on the southern bank (of the Kāvēri).
Published in S.I.I. Vol. VIII No. 654.

l.ta:
teṭakki ulakan vaitta nel aimpatin kalam kalattuvāyt tiṅkaḷ
irunāḻi palicai

inscription.head:
No. 54.
(A.R. No. of 1998-99.)
VĀYALŌGAM, KULATTUR TALUK,
PUDUKOTTAI DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On broken stones (three pieces) kept at the Saptamātrikā temple near the tank.

Records the gift of two plots of land amounting to three mā, nāṭṭār-peruñchey
and nāṟṟukkāl tuḍarchchey for the maintenance of the tank (kuḷappaṭṭi) at Vayalōkam
in Vayalaka-nāḍu by the sword regiment Vīrachōḷa Ilaṅgōvēḷār (Vīrachōḻa Ilaṅgōvēḷār-
vāḷpaḍai) and the body-guard unit uḍanilai-chēvagar. It is also stated that these two
plots of land were made over to the tank perpetually.
Published in Āvaṇam Vol. No. 10 (1999) p. 15.

inscription.head:
No. 55.
(A.R. No. 243 of 1944.)
VĀLIKAṆḌAPURAM, PERAMBALUR TALUK,
TIRUCHIRAPPALLI DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the north wall of the central shrine in the Vālikaṇḍēśvara temple.

This record on the grounds of palaeography may be assigned to the tenth
century. Possibly it belongs to the reign period of Rājakēsarivarmaṉ Sundarachōḷa.

It records that Maṟavaṉ Tuṅgāṉaiyān Pirāntaka-Vāḷanāḍuḍiyāṉ and his brother-
in-law Vāṇarāyaṉ Araviñjaṉ Rājādittan had a contest (koḻiporutti) in which the latter
was defeated and that he entrusted 7 pure gold (sempoṉ) to the body, Saṅkarapāḍi-
nagarattār in Vālikaṇḍapuram for burning a perpetual lamp to the deity Tiruvālīśvarattu-
Paramēśvarar for the merit of Pirāntaka-Vaḷanāḍuḍiyāṉ, with an uḻakku of ghee
daily. Araviñjaṇ Irājādittaṉ seems to have presented a copper-lamp for the purpose.

l.ta: yaṉ araviñcaṉ irācātittaṉun taṅkaḷiṟ koḻiporutti araviñ-
caṉ irācātittaṉ ṟoṟṟu pirānta vaḷaṉāṭuṭaiyāṉukkākat tiru-
ivviḷakkukku vālikaṇṭapurattuc caṅkarapāṭi nakarattār vacam ku-
ceppuviḷakku oṉṟu ivviḷakki(l)le iravum pakalum erikka

inscription.head:
No. 56.
(A.R. No. 238 of 1944.)
VĀLIKAṆḌAPURAM, PERAMBALUR TALUK,
TIRUCHIRAPPALLI DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the north wall of the central shrine in the Vālikaṇḍēśvara temple.

It records an undertaking given by the body called Saṅkarapāḍi-nagarattār of
the village Vālikaṇḍapuram to contribute one uḻakku of ghee measured by nārāyam
to burn a perpetual lamp for god Paramēśvara in the temple of Tiruvālīśvaram in
Vālikaṇḍapuram in Vaṉnāḍu after accepting 7(1/2) kaḻañju of pure gold weighed by
the nagarakkal deposited for the purpose by Mādaṉ Vaikunthan alias
Raṇaraṅgarāgappallavaraiyaṉ of Puliyūr in Vaṉ-nāḍu.

l.ta: ṇaraṅkarākap pallavaraiyaṉ vaitta nontā viḷakku o-
ṉṟukku ivvūril nakarakallāl cempoṉ eḻu kaḻai-
ñcaraiyum koṇṭu nārāyattāluḻak keṇṇai iravum paka-
lum aṭṭuvomivvūr caṅkarappāṭi nakarattom itu cantirā-

l.note:
The two letters riya are found engraved above the line over the letters of tta in the beṅinning
of line no. 8.

inscription.head:
No. 57.
(A.R. No. 246 of 1944.)
VĀLIKAṆḌAPURAM, PERAMBALUR TALUK,
TIRUCHIRAPPALLI DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the north wall of the central shrine in the Vālikaṇḍēśvara temple.

This inscription on the grounds of palaeography may be assigned to the tenth
century A.D. It may belong to the reign period of Rājakēsarivarman Sundarachōḷa.

It records that Achchaṉakkaṉ, a servant belonging to the sword regiment called
Iḷaiya-Koṅga-vāḷ (of Vālikaṇḍapuram) made a gift of seven kaḷañju of gold for
burning a perpetual lamp to the god Mahādēva of Tiruvālīśvaram in Vālikaṇḍapuram
in Vaṉ-nāḍu. The gift-gold was entrusted to the Saṅkarappāḍi-nagarattār, with the
stipulation that they should supply one uḻakku (of oil) daily by the measure nārāyam
(for burning the lamp).

l.ta: accaṉakkaṉ vaitta nontā viḷakku+itiṉukku poṉ eḻu kaḻañcu

ippoṉṉā[l*] nārāyattāl nica[ti*] uḻakku aṭṭakkaṭavar caṅkarapāṭi
nakara[t*]tā[r*]

edition.l: itu canti[rā]tittavalam pamhēśvarakṣa Read panmāhēśvararakṣai[||*]

inscription.head:
No. 58.
(A.R. No. 12 of 1896.)
KARIKAL, ARAKONAM TALUK,
NORTH ARCOT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On a slab which forms a roof of the Ponni-Amman temple.

Records that Āchchan Veṇṇāyi Naṅgai, of Eṟupāḍi in Kiḻār-kūṟṟam, the wife of
Vīravaḷañjiyaṉ of Maṟiyāḍi in Pulivala-nāḍu, had caused the construction of the
temple for Piḍāri (Bhaṭṭāraki).
Published in Ep. Ind. Vol. IV. p. 331.

inscription.head:
No. 59.
(A.R. No. 315 of 1911.)
VĒḶACHCHĒRI, SAIDAPET TALUK,
CHINGLEPUT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the south wall, central shrine in the Daṇḍīśvara temple.

This inscription has been assigned to Sundarachōḷa. It records that Dēvakumāra
Kramavittan of Teṉṉūr, one of the āḷuṅgaṇattār of Vēḷichchēri in Kōṭṭūr-nāḍu
(a sub-division) of Puliyūr-kōṭṭam made a gift of ninety sheep for burning a
perpetual lamp to Tirutaṇḍīśvara-dēvar of the above village.
Published in S.I.I. Vol. III. No. 114.

l.ta:
cāvatu puliyūr koṭṭa[t*]tu ko[ṭ*]ṭur nāṭṭu veḷi[c*]ceri
yāḷuṅka-

inscription.head:
No. 60.
(A.R. No. 246 of 1912.)
TIRUVOṞṞIYŪR, SAIDAPET TALUK,
CHINGLEPUT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On a pillar lying to the south of the Ādhipurīśvara temple.

This inscription though belonging to Sundarachōḷa refers to Uttamachōḷa. It
records that an individual Kaḍuttalai Nāgamaiyaṉ, son of Śiṅgamaiyaṉ, a Kalēśi
perundaram official, who had accompanied uḍaiyār Uttamachōḷa, donated ninety
sheep for burning one perpetual lamp and also an Īḻa-lamp to Tiruvoṟṟiyūr Mahādēva.
Published in S.I.I. Vol. III. No. 115.

l.ta: ṭu toṇṇūṟu īḻaviḷakku onṟu itu pan

inscription.head:
No. 61.
(A.R. No. 274 of 1978.)
TIRUMALAI, POLUR TALUK, NORTH ARCOT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: Rock near the steps leading to the temple.

This registers a gift of 95 sheep for burning a perpetual lamp to the god of
Tirumalai, a hamlet of Vaigavūr in Paṅgaḷa-nāḍu by Ēkavīraṉ Daṉmasaṅgātiyār alias
Malaiyamahādēviyār, the wife of Malāḍu-uḍaiya Nāṭṭāṉ Sittavaḍattaḍigaḷ of Bhārgava
gōtra and the daughter of Nāḍāḷvār Iḷaṅgōṉaḍigaḷ.

l.ta:
viyār nāṭāḷvār iḷaṅkoṉaṭikaḷ makaḷār ekavīraṉ
taṉmacaṅkātiyā-

ṅ keṭuppāṉ kaṅkaiiṭaik kumariyiṭaic
ceytār ceyta pāvaṅ koḷvā-

inscription.head:
No. 62.
(A.R. No. 291 of 1908.)
TIRUKKAḶITTAṬṬAI, KUMBAHKONAM TALUK,
TANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the north wall of the central shrine in the Vēdapurīśvara temple.

p.b: Sundarachōḷa, who drove the Pāṇḍya into the forest Year 7: 964 A.D.

inscription.p:
It records that Pirāntaka Siṟiyavēḷān alias Tirukkaṟṟaḷi-Pichchaṉ, who was serving
as a general of the king purchased a plot of land called taḷi-viḷāgam, half-a-vēḷi in
extent, and also got it freed from taxes with 156 kaḷañju of gold, he had endowed
for providing ritualistic offerings of food for the god Śrī Kuḍittiṭṭai uḍaiyār at
Vembaṟṟūr on the northern bank of the (river Kāvēri).
Published in Ep. Ind. Vol. XII. p. 126.

l.ta: ñcuṅ kuṭuttu koṇṭu iṟaiyili cetu koṭutta nilamāvatu it

ttukku vaṭakku taḷiviḷākam [e*]nṟu per kūvappaṭṭa nilam araiyum
it

vaṟ celvatāka iṟaiyiḻicci kuṭutten pirāntakan
ciṟiya veḷāna(n)āna tirukkaṟṟaḷi piccananeṉ [||*]

inscription.head:
No. 63.
(A.R. No. 232 of 1942.)
TIRUKKAṆḌIYŪR, TANJAVUR TALUK,
TANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On a pillar lying in the compound of the ruined Īśvara temple.

This inscription on the grounds of palaeography may be assigned to the tenth
century A.D., as it may belong to the reign-period of Rājakēsarivarmaṉ Sundarachōḷa.

It records the definition of the boundaries of a plot of land, 1/8 of a vēli in
extent, belonging to the god who was pleased to keep standing. Among the
boundaries of the land two plots of strīdhana land owned by the wife of Sāvāndi
Śrīkaṇṭaṉ, is mentioned.

inscription.head:
No. 64.
(A.R. No. 382 of 1924.)
MĒLAPPAḺUVŪR, UDAIYARPALAIYAM TALUK,
TIRUCHIRAPPALLI DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the south wall of the Chōḷēśvara shrine in the Agastyēśvara temple.

p.note:
The identity of this Rājakēsarivarman with Sundarachōḷa is doubtful. This Rājakēsarivarman could
be Rājarāja I also.

inscription.p:
This inscription refers to an endowment made in the 9th regnal year of
Rājakēsarivarman (Sundarachōḷa) but it was given effect in the subsequent period ie
during the reign of Uttamachōḷa.

It records that Vīrachōḻa Aṇukkaṉ Śiṟiyappi Maḻapāḍi of Avanigandharvvapuram
made a gift of ten kaḻañju of gold for burning a perpetual lamp with one uḻakku
of ghee daily to the shrine (situated) to the north of the temple of Mahādēva of
Avanigandharvva-Īśvaragriham, a dēvadāna in Kuṉṟa-kkūṟṟam. During the sixteenth
regnal year of Uttamachōḷa on the orders of Paḻuvēṭṭaraiyar Kaṇḍaṉ Maṟavaṉ, the
two nagarattārs who were examining the śrīkāriyam of the temple received the
gold for interest (ie supplying ghee for burning the lamp). Probably later, when the
śrīkāriyam of the temple was being checked by Kauśikan Nakkan Māṟapirāṉ, the
gold was taken over by the paṭṭuḍiyār-eḻuvar (seven priests, possibly of Avani-
gandharvva-Īśvaragriham), for arranging to burn the perpetual lamp.
Published in S.I.I. Vol. XIII No. 201.

veṭṭaraiyar kaṇṭaṉ maṟavaṉār aru[ḷi*] ceyya śrī kā

inscription.head:
No. 65.
(A.R. No. 267 of 1911.)
TILLAISTHĀNAM, TANJAVUR TALUK,
TANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the south wall in the Central shrine in the Ghritasthanēśvara temple.

Records two gifts of 25 kaḻañju of gold each by Teṉṉavan Pritimārāśan alias
Kaṭṭi Ūraṉ and Varaguṇa Perumaṉār, the queen of Parāntaka Iḷaṅgōvēḷār for
burning two perpetual lamps with two uḻakku of oil daily to the god, Mahādēva at
Tiruneittānam in Poygai-nāḍu. With the 50 kaḻañju of gold the sabhaiyār of
Tiruneittānam and pādamūlattār agreed to burn the two perpetual lamps daily by
reclaiming ten chey of temple land (earmarked for this purpose). Among the
boundaries of land, a plot of land owned by a Vishṇu temple is mentioned.
Published in S.I.I. Vol. III No. 113.

inscription.head:
No. 66.
(A.R. No. 557 of 1920.)
UḌAIYĀRKUḌI, CHIDAMBARAM TALUK,
SOUTH ARCOT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the south wall of the central shrine in the Anantēśvarasvāmin temple.

p.b: Parakēsarivarman, who took the head of Vīrapāṇḍya (Āditya II) Year 2: 962 A.D.

inscription.p:
Records an undertaking given by the temple officials (dēvakanmigaḷ) to Araiyaṉ
Gēyaviṭaṅkaṉ, a member of (kaikōḷa) regiment, Siṅgaḷāntaka-tteriñja-kaikkōḷar to
provide a pair of cloth-wreaths (tirupparisaṭṭam) to the god Mahādēva who is
dancing in the stone temple, Tiruvanantiśvaram at Śrī Vīranārāyaṇa-chaturvēdimaṅgalam,
a brahmadēyam on the northern bank (of the river Kāvēri), with the interest of
three kāśu received from him.

edition.l: āṭi arukiṉṟa m[ ādēvar]

inscription.head:
No. 67.
(A.R. No. 223 of 1915.)
BRAHMADĒSAM, CHEYYAR TALUK,
NORTH ARCOT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the south wall of the central shrine in the Chandramauḷīśvara temple.

p.b: Mahārāja, who took the head of Vīrapāṇḍya (Āditya II) Year 2: 962 A.D.

inscription.p:
Records that an individual Iḷanāṅgūr alias Alaṅkāracheṭṭi, the son of Maṅgalapriyaṉ
Kalivilaṅgi, a merchant of Tiruvēgambachchēri in Rājamalla-chaturvēdimaṅgalam
endowed fifteen kaḻañju of gold for burning a perpetual lamp to Śrī Pōndai-
pperumānaḍigaḷ. The gaṇapperumakkaḷ of the temple (who were entrusted with the
gold gifted) were to burn the lamp out of the interest on the gifted amount.

iḷanāṅkūr ākiya ala-
ṉaiṅ kaḻañcu ippoṉ patiṉaiṅ kaḻañcālum vanta

aṟamallatu tu-

inscription.head:
No. 68.
(A.R. No. 215 of 1985.)
BĀHŪR, PONDICHERRY.
inscription.p:
On the east wall, ardhamaṇḍapa in front of the central shrine, in the
Mūlasthānēśvara temple.

p.b: Parakēsari, who took the head of Vīrapāṇḍya (Āditya II) Year 3: 963 A.D.

inscription.p:
It records a gift of ninetysix sheep by Kaliyaṉ Maṉṟāḍi Aiyyaṉ alias
Āchārakāntappallavaraiyaṉ of Karai in Nerumūr-nāḍu in Chembūr-kkōṭṭam for
burning a perpetual lamp to the god Śrī-Mūlasthānattu Mahādēva at Vāgūr. He also
donated a lamp-stand for burning this lamp.

l.ta: nerumurnāṭṭuk karai uṭai[yaṉ] kaliyaṉ maṉṟāṭi aiyya-

inscription.head:
No. 69.
(A.R. No. 246 of 1969.)
BĒLŪR, SALEM TALUK, SALEM DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On a stone set up outside the Tāṉtoṉṟīśvara temple.

p.b: Parakēsari, who took the head of Vīrapāṇḍya (Āditya II) Year 3: 963 A.D.

inscription.p:
Records that Milāḍudaiyāṉ Akaḷaṅkaṉ Malaiyar Ādittan alias Sēmbiyaṉ Milāḍuḍaiyāṉ
of Bhārghava gōtra has authorized the administrators of Tuṟivi-nāḍu (Tuṟivi-nāṭṭu-
ūrāḷigaḷ) to collect four and quarter kaḻañju of gold as tax towards ūrāḷittiṟai.

inscription.head:
No. 70.
(A.R. No. 380 of 1938.)
ŚĪṞUPĀKKAM, TIRUKKOYILUR TALUK,
SOUTH ARCOT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On a rock near the Śekkaḍippāṟai.

p.b: Parakēsarivarman, who took the head of Vīrapāṇḍya (Āditya II) Year 3: 960 A.D.

inscription.p:
The inscription is ruled between the lines. Records that the ūrār and the kō (the
land lord) assigned a plot of three mā of land as ērippaṭṭi (evidently for the
maintenance of lake of the village).

l.note: The letter pa is engraved like ma
The letter e is engraved like 100

inscription.head:
No. 71.
(A.R. No. 233 of 1911.)
KUMBHAKŌṆAM, KUMBHAKONAM TALUK, TANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the north wall of the central shrine in the Nāgēśvarasvāmin temple.

p.b: Parakēsarivarman, who took the head of Pāṇḍya (Āditya II) Year 3: 963 A.D.

inscription.p:
Records that the mūlaparuḍai perumakkaḷ (members of the temple committe) of
Tirukkūḍamukkil, a dēvadāna in Pāmbūr-nāḍu on the northern bank (of the river
Kāvēri), sold two mā of land out of 24 vēli which they obtained as gift from the
king on the occasion of (his) coronation ceremony, to Kōyil Mayilai alias Parāntaka
Mūvēndavēḷāṉ of Siṟṟiṅgaṉ in Iṅganāḍu. The latter set apart the land, (thus purchased)
as bhaṭṭavritti for the person who expounds prabhākaram. One person named
Parānṭakan figures as signatory.
Published in S.I.I. Vol. III No. 200.

l.note: The length of nā is not marked by the usual kāl but is added as in the case of ṟā and ṉā
The length of nā is not marked by the usual kāl but is added as in the case of ṟā and ṉā

edition.l: veḷāṉeṉ itu āyirantiruvaṭiyuṭaiy[ ār ra]-

inscription.head:
No. 72.
(A.R. No. 57 of 1995-96.)
VEṄKAGAṬṬŪṚ, GUDIYATTAM TALUK, NORTH ARCOT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On a hero-stone set up in a mango groove owned by Doraisamy Naidu.

p.b: Parakēsarivarman, who took the head of Vīrapāṇḍya (Āditya II) Year 3: 963 A.D.

inscription.p:
Records that when the village Chōḷaṉūr was destroyed and its cattle was seized,
Śāttayaṉ, the son of Vikramādittan alias Diṉmacheṭṭi, a sluice operator at Cheṅguṉṟam
fought with that cattle lifter and died while rescuring the cattle.

l.ta: ḷḷak koṭṭu pūcal poyt toṟu mi-

inscription.head:
No. 73.
(A.R. No. 58 of 1995-96.)
VEṄKAGAṬṬŪṚ, GUDIYATTAM TALUK, NORTH ARCOT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On another hero-stone set up in the same place.

p.b: Parakēsarivarman, who took the head of Vīrapāṇḍya (Āditya II) Year 3: 963 A.D.

inscription.p:
Records that when the village Tiruvūṟal Chōḷaṉūr was destroyed and its cattle
were lifted Śāttayaṉ, the sluice-operator at Cheṅguṉṟam fought with that cattle lifter
and died while rescuing the cattle.

inscription.head:
No. 74.
(A.R. No. 610 of 1920.)
UḌAIYĀGUḌI, CHIDAMBARAM TALUK, SOUTH ARCOT DISTRICT.
inscription.p:
On the outerbase of the south wall of the maṇḍapa in front of the central
shrine in the Anantēśvara temple.

p.b: Parakēsarivarman, who took the head of Vīrapāṇḍya (Āditya II) Year 4: 964 A.D.

inscription.p:
Records the endowment made by Kāḷaiyaṉ Kōḍaṉ, who was a sāma-nāyakaṉ in
the military unit, mūtta paḻaiya vāḷ, on the day of Kārttigai in the month of
Makara coinciding with Monday in the year cited, for feeding a brāhmaṇa at
uttamāgram (daily). For this purpose he had entrusted to the gaṇapperumakkaḷ, who
were administering the temple, thirty five and half kaḻañju of gold. He had also
donated vessels, taḷigai and vaṭṭil for preparing the food. Details of the items of food
to be provided are given in this record.

vat nicati nellum patiṉāḻiyum

inscription.head:
No. 75.
(A.R. No. 574 of 1971.)
TAÑJĀVŪR, TANJAVUR TALUK, TANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p:
Stray stones collected and kept in the south-east verandah of the first prākāra in
the Brihadīśvara temple.

p.b: Parakēsarivarman, who took the head of Vīrapāṇḍya (Āditya II) Year 4: 964 A.D.

inscription.p:
Seems to record the investigation (ārāchchi) of the affairs of the Taḷikkuḷa-dēvar
temple by Parāntaka Mūvēndavēḷār. In the course of this investigation it appears to
have been found that 12 kāśu endowed by Pichchaṉ Vikramābharaṇi, a servant
(peṇḍāṭṭi) residing at Ponnamaraiyar-aṅgaḍi for a perpetual lamp during the 11th
regnal year of Rājakēsarivarmaṉ (Sundarachōḷa) still remained unallotted for earning
interest to maintain the lamp. This amount was now entrusted to Nāgarāśaṉ
Tiruvaḍigaḷ of the same aṅgaḍi after obtaining a hand receipt.

l.ta:
ḷāṉārācci taḷikkuḷatevarkku poṉṉamaraiyar aṅkāṭi irukkum
poṇṭāṭṭi piccaṉ vikkiramāparaṇi
nākarācaṉ tiruvaṭikaḷ kaiyyeḻuttolai iṭṭu koṇṭa kācu

inscription.head:
No. 76.
(A.R. No. 248 of 1937.)
TIMMICHCHŪR, TIRUKKOYILUR TALUK,
SOUTH ARCOT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On a boulder known as Taḍaṅgal near the Śiva temple.

p.b: Parakēsarivarman, who took the head of Pāṇḍya (Āditya II) Year 5: 965 A.D.

inscription.p:
Seems to record the gift of two plots of land as ambalappaṭṭi by Munmuḍi
chōḻa Kāḍupaṭṭi of Timiśūr in Timiśūr-nāḍu for cleaning and also for providing fire-
wood to the firehearth (tīppātti) in the hall constructed by himself. He also donated
sheep for burning a perpetual lamp in the same hall. The inscription further records
that he also constructed a shrine for Piḍāriyār and consecrated the goddess, images
there.

l.note:
His full name is given as mummuṭiccoḻak kāṭupaṭṭi in line no. 8. Hence this may be restored
to mummuṭic coḻakkāṭupaṭṭi

part.p:
PART-II
TEXTS OF THE INSCRIPTIONS OF
UTTAMACHOLA

inscription.head:
No. 1.
(A.R. No. 287 of 1917.)
KŪHŪR, KUMBHAKONAM TALUK, TANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the west and south walls, central shrine, Amaravanēśvara temple.

This inscription appears to be a later copy (engraved in Rājarāja I's period).
It is damaged in several places and the writing is also not very clear. It seems to
refer to the grant of several plots of land to the god of Kūrur in Tirunaṟaiyūr-nāḍu
in Kshatriyasikhāmaṇi-vaḷanāḍu. It is enjoined to apportion the yield of land for
paying wages to several service personnel of the temple. A piece of land earmarked
for conducting offerings and worship (archanābhōgam) to the god Tiruveḷḷarukkīsvaram-
uḍaiyār is mentioned.

inscription.head:
No. 2.
(A.R. No. 540 of 1920.)
UḌAIYĀRKUḌI, CHIDAMBARAM TALUK, SOUTH ARCOT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the south wall, central shrine, Anantēśvarasvāmi temple.

This might be assigned to Uttama chōḷa N. Sethuraman, Early Cholas: Mathematics reconstructs the chronology, pp. 38-39.. This records a gift of ninety-six
sheep and a ram for a perpetual lamp in the temple of Tiruvanantēśvarattāḻvār at
Vīranārāyaṇa-chaturvēdimaṅgalam by Parāntakan Mādēvaḍigaḷ alias Sembiyan MAdēviyār,
the daughter of Maḻavaraiyar, and queen of Gaṇḍarādityadēvar, who was pleased to
go west ie deceased.
Published in S.I.I., Vol. XIX. No. 11.

l.ta: yar makaḷār parāntakaṉ mātevaṭikaḷāṉa cempiyaṉ māte-

edition.l:
vārkku vaiytta nonta viḷakku 1- kku vaitta vāṭu 96 takar
1 [||*]-

inscription.head:
No. 3.
(A.R. No. 552 of 1920.)
UḌAIYĀRKUḌI, CHIDAMBARAM TALUK, SOUTH ARCOT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the south wall, central shrine, Anantēśvaraśvāmi temple.

Records that Aiyyāṟaṉ Śēndaṉ Nikaḷaṅga Mūvēndavēḷāṉ of Sāttappāḍi in
Vēśālippāḍi gifted 12(1/2) kaḻañju of gold for burning a perpetual lamp to the god,
Tiruvanantēśvaratt-āḷvār at Śrī Vīranārāyaṇa-chaturvēdimaṅgalam. The shepherds
who received the gold agreed to burn the lamp with one uḻakku of oil daily after
acquiring 96 sheep and a ram.
Published in S.I.I., Vol. XIX No. 14.

l.ta: poṉ paṉṉiru kaḻañcarai ippoṉ koṇṭu cāvā

edition.l: muvāpperāṭu 94- m takar 1- m koṇṭu nicatam uḻa-

inscription.head:
No. 4.
(A.R. No. 558 of 1920.)
UḌAIYĀRKUḌI, CHIDAMBARAM TALUK, SOUTH ARCOT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the south wall, central shrine, Anantēśvaraśvāmi temple.

p.b: Year: 2, Tula, Sunday, Jyēshṭha: 972 A.D. Oct. 13 MUla is wrong for JyesTha 1.
p.note: N. Sethuraman, Early Cholas: Mathermatics reconstructs the chronology, P. 17.

inscription.p:
This records a gift of 12(1/2) kaḻañju of gold for a perpetual lamp in the temple
of Tiruvanantīśvaram-Paramasvāmi by Echchil Sadurākaraṉ alias Teṉṉavaṉ Miṟai-
nāṭṭu Mūvēndavēḷāṉ of Dēvaṉguḍi in Miṟai-kkūṟṟam on the northern bank. The
amount was left in charge of the mahāsabhā of the village. The last line which is
damaged seems to have recorded some other gift by the same donor to the deity
Kūttaperumāḷ (Naṭarāja).
Published in S.I.I., Vol. XIX No. 16.

inscription.head:
No. 5.
(A.R. No. 144 of 1928.)
TIRUPPAḺANAM, TANJAVUR TALUK, TANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the north wall, central shrine, Āpatsahāyēśvara temple.

Incomplete. Damaged in several places. It records that an individual Kaṟṟaḷi
Bhaṭṭālagaṉ, a member of the (regiment) Arumoḻidēva-teriñja kaikōḷar purchased
several plots of dry-crop land at Pēraṅgālakkuḍi, a dēvadānam in Miṟai-kkūṟṟam
from the Ādichaṇḍēśvarar (the god-secretary of the temple) and padipādamūlattār
(the worshipping priest of the sanctum) and endowed the same after reclaiming them,
for feeding four brāhmaṇas daily, in the temple at Tiruppaḻanam, a dēvadānam in
Miṟai-kkūṟṟam.
Published in S.I.I., Vol. XIX No. 29.

inscription.head:
No. 6.
(A.R. No. 136 of 1926.)
TIRUVIḶAKKUḌI, MAYURAM TALUK, TANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the south wall, central shrine, Maṇavāḷēśvara temple.

p.b: Year 2: Mēsha, Sunday, Tiruvōṇam, Sunday is wrong for Monday
p.note: N. Sethuraman, Early Cholas: Mathermatics reconstructs the Chronology, P. 17.

inscription.p:
The inscription is built in at the right end. It records the remission of taxes on
some plots of land belonging to the temple at Tiruvēḷvikkuḍi, which had been
purchased from several persons by the peruṅguṟi-pperumakkaḷ of Viḍēlviḍugu-
chchaturvēdimaṅgalam, a brahmadēyam in Kurukkai-nāḍu on the northern bank of
the river, on receiving a lump-sum of 50 īḻākkāśu from a member of the Karikāla-
chōḷa-teriñja-kaikkōḷar. As a post-script to this inscription the land called Śrī-Karaṇa-
peruviḷāgam reclaimed by madhyastha of Viḍēlviḍugu-chaturvēdimaṅgalam and the
specific services for which the yield from that land was to be utilized are mentioned.
Published in S.I.I., Vol. XIX No. 27.

inscription.head:
No. 7.
(A.R. No. 602 of 1920.)
UḌAIYĀRKUḌI, CHIDAMBARAM TALUK, SOUTH ARCOT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the north wall, central shrine, Anantēśvarasvāmi temple.

This records a gift of 12(1/2) kaḻañju of gold for burning a perpetual lamp
before the deity Chandraśēkhara perumāḷ by Pañchavanmādēvi, the consort of
Araiyan Mahimālayaṉ alias Parāntaka Muttaraiyaṉ. The shepherd community
(maṉṟāḍikkalaṉai) of the place received the amount and undertook to supply an
uḻakku of ghee daily for the purpose.
Published in S.I.I., Vol. XIX No. 61.

inscription.head:
No. 8.
(A.R. No. 42 of 1995-96.)
TIRUVIŚALŪR, KUMBHAKONAM TALUK, TANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the west wall, central shrine, Śivayōganāthaśvāmin temple.

Incomplete. Records that (the king) Parakēsarivarman alias śrī Gaṇḍarādittan
Madhurāntakan donated kāśu in order to conduct the sacred bath every month on
saṅkrānthi day with 108 pots (of water) to the god, Mahādēvar of Tiruviśalūr in
Avaṉinārāyaṇa-chaturvēdimaṅgalam, a dēvadāna-brahmadēyam on the northern bank.
With that kāśu plots of land were purchased from several individuals in the name
of the god (for conducting the above ceremony).
Published in Varalāṟu issue No. 5, August 1995. PP 5-6.

edition.l: svastiśrī [||*] kopparakesaripanma[rk*]ki yāṇṭu 2- vatiṉ etir ām

inscription.head:
No. 9.
(A.R. No. 165 of 1928.)
TIRUPPAḺANAM, TANJAVUR TALUK, TANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the south wall, central shrine, Āpatsahāyēśvara temple.

This records a gift of 30 kaḻañju of gold for burning a perpetual lamp in the
temple of Mahādēva with the interest thereon, by Nakkaṉ Ariñjigai alias Parāntaka-
Pallava[raiyaṉ]. The gold was taken over by the nagarattār of Tiruppaḻaṉam.
Published in S.I.I., Vol. XIX No. 70.

inscription.head:
No. 10.
(A.R. No. 330 of 1958-59.)
CHIDAMBARAM, CHIDAMBARAM TALUK, SOUTH ARCOT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: Broken stones in the nandavana of Anantēśvara temple in the same town.

Fragment. In characters of the tenth century A.D. Seems to refer to the gift
of sixty kaḻañju of gold for feeding at the Tiruvanantēśvara temple.

inscription.head:
No. 11.
(A.R. No. 226 of 1940.)
TIRUNĀMANALLŪR, TIRUKKOYILUR TALUK,
SOUTH ARCOT DISTRICT.
inscription.p:
On the north wall of the maṇḍapa in front of the central shrine,
Bhaktajanēśvara temple.

Records that twenty-four śeṟuvu of land (spreading) in four territorial divisions
(sīmai) donated by Iruṇīkki Aḍaiyakkōṉ to the deity Tiruttoṇḍīśvaratt-āḻvār, is
set-apart for maintaining five persons, cleaning the floors and walls of the temple.

l.ta:
kappaṭṭa nilam iriṇikki aṭaiyakoṉ vakaiyāy ceṟuvu
irupattaiñcil loru mākā[ṇi]k kāḷaṅkaikku nikki niṉṟa ceṟuvu

inscription.head:
No. 12.
(A.R. No. 134 of 1916.)
MĪÑJŪR, PONNERI TALUK, CHINGLEPUT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the south wall, central shrine, Varadarāja-perumāḷ temple.

This inscription is in verse. It states that the temple of Sōḻakula sundara-viṇṇagar
was constructed at Miññūr by an individual Kēśavaṉ Karukaikkōṉ for the god
Āḻivalak-Kēśava.

inscription.head:
No. 13.
(A.R. No. 417 of 1993.)
GŌPURAPPAṬṬI, LALGUDI TALUK, TIRUCHIRAPPALLI DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the south wall, mukha-maṇḍapa of Tirumēṟṟaḷīśvara temple.

Damaged. Seems to refer to some gift made to the deity, Tirumēṟṟaḷi uḍaiyār,
at Pāchchil in Maḻa-nāḍu by a dancing girl (talaikkōli) of Karaipputtūr.

inscription.head:
No. 14.
(A.R. No. 114 of 1926.)
TIRUVIḶAKKUḌI, MAYURAM TALUK, TANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the north wall, central shrine, Maṇavāḷēśvara temple.

Records a gift of a silver plate weighing 400 kaḻañju as weighed by the ūrkkāl
to god Tiruvēḻvikkuḍi-Mahādēva, by Ayyaṉ Mārasiṅgaṉ alias Vīraśēkhara-Mūvēndavēḷāṉ
of Guṇamalaippāḍi in Nallāṟṟūr-nāḍu on the northern bank of the river Kāvēri. The
body śrī-karaṇattār was entrusted with the protection of this benefaction.
Published in S.I.I., Vol. XIX No. 107.

edition.l: kopparakesaripanmaṟku yāṇṭu 4- vatu

inscription.head:
No. 15.
(A.R. No. 124 of 1926.)
TIRUVIḶAKKUḌI, MAYURAM TALUK, TANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the south wall, central shrine, Maṇavāḷēśvara temple.

This records a gift of 36 sheep for burning a perpetual lamp to god
Tiruvēḷvikkuḍi-uḍaiyār by Koṟṟaṉ Kūttaṉ alias Sembiyaṉ Ārkkāṭṭuvēḷāṉ of Ārkkāḍu
in Ārkkāṭṭu-kūṟṟam.
Published in S.I.I., Vol. XIX No. 108.

edition.l: ṇṭu 4- vatu ārkāṭṭukkūṟ[ṟa*]t-

inscription.head:
No. 16.
(A.R. No. 125 of 1926.)
TIRUVIḶAKKUḌI, MAYURAM TALUK, TANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the south wall, central shrine, Maṇavāḷēśvara temple.

Incomplete. Records a gift of two kāśu by an individual, Paṉaiyaṉ Kōdaṇḍaṉ,
a kaikkōḷa of Iḷaiya-vāḷ army unit to the god of Tiruvēḷvikkuḍi.

edition.l: svastiśrī [||*] kopparakecaripa[ nmak] ku yāṇ[ṭu*]

inscription.head:
No. 17.
(A.R. No. 373 of 1924.)
MĒLAPPAḺUVŪR, UDAIYARPALAIYAM TALUK,
TIRUCHIRAPPALLI DISTRICT.
inscription.p:
On the east wall of the maṇḍapa in front of the Agastyēśvara shrine,
Agastyēśvara temple.

Much damaged. It seems to record a gift of ten kaḻañju of gold for a
perpetual lamp in the temple of Mahādēva of Avanigandharva-Īśvara-gṛiham, a
dēvadāṉam in Kuṉṟa-kkūṟṟam by Atiśūraṉ •••• of Maḻapāḍi in [Vā]ṇagoppāḍi. He
deposited the amount with two nagarattār each with five poṉ. The inscription also
refers to a later order, the nature of which is not known, of Aḍigaḷ Paḻuvēṭṭaraiyar
Kaṇḍaṉ [Amuda]nār made in the [13]th year of Uttamachōḻa, evidently about the
utilization of this amount.
Published in S.I.I., Vol. XIX No. 105.

ṇṭu palicai iṭṭu v[ā*]rā niṟkka śrī uttamacoḻa ••• r koppara
kecaripa-

inscription.head:
No. 18.
(A.R. No. 404 of 1903.)
TIRUVĀMĀTTŪR, VILUPPURAM TALUK,
SOUTH ARCOT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the west wall, central shrine, Abhiramēśvara temple.

Records that Gaṇḍarādittan alias Vānavaṉ Mahārājan of Vāṇagōppāḍi gifted a
perpetual lamp for God Mahādēvar of Tiruvāmāttūr. For burning this lamp one
uḻakku of ghee was stipulated to be contributed half by sabhaiyār and ūrār of
Tiruvāmāttur and the other half by the managers of the temple-out of the interest
on the total of twelve kaḻañju of gold endowed by (the above stated) chief which
had been shared equally by the two classes of administrative bodies mentioned above.
Published in S.I.I., Vol. VIII No. 720.

inscription.head:
No. 19.
(A.R. No. 245 of 1911.)
KUMBHAKŌṆAM, KUMBHAKONAM TALUK, TANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the West and South walls, central shrine, Nāgēśvarasvāmin temple.

p.b: Year 4 Mēsha, Thursday, Navami Maghā = 975 A.D., April 22.

inscription.p:
Damaged in several places. Registers a sale of quarter chey of land by the
temple-administrative body, Mūlaparaḍai-perumakkaḷ of Tirukkuḍamukkil in Pāmbūr-
nāḍu, to a lady, Kaṟṟaḷippirāṭṭi Aṟivāḷaṉmitravalli, a resident of Tañjāvūr in Tañjāvūr-
kūṟṟam for 25 kaḻañju of gold. The lady in turn endowed the land to the temple,
for burning a perpetual lamp to the deity Tirukkīḻkkōṭṭattu-paramasvāmi, for the
merit of her son Kārikoḻambaṉ, a member of Gaṇḍarāditta-teriñja-kaikkōḷar. The
land sold by the mūlaparuḍai-perumakkaḷ to the lady is said to have been the
abhishēka-dakshiṇai received by the assembly evidently on the occasion of the king's
coronation. The mūlapparuḍaipperumakkaḷ had authorized the temple śrīkāriyam to
burn the perpetual lamp with the amount of paddy received as bhōgam for
the land.
Published in S.I.I., Vol. XIX No. 95.

inscription.head:
No. 20.
(A.R. No. 243 of 1911.)
KUMBHAKŌṆAM, KUMBHAKONAM TALUK, TANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the West and South walls of the central shrine, Nāgēśvara temple.

Damaged and incomplete. It seems to record the distribution of the sheep
which had been presented to the temple of Tirukkīḻkōṭṭattu-paramasvāmi at
Tirukkuḍamukkil in Pāmbūr-nāḍu, among several shepherds, for the supply of the
necessary ghee by them for burning perpetual lamps in the temple.
Published in S.I.I., Vol. XIX No. 132.

inscription.head:
No. 21.
(A.R. No. 244 of 1911.)
KUMBHAKŌṆAM, KUMBHAKONAM TALUK,
TANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the West and South walls of the central shrine, Nāgēśvara temple.

Records a gift of 97 sheep for burning a perpetual lamp in the temple of
Tirukkīḻkōṭṭattu-Mahādēva by a shepherd named Dēvaṉ Nakkaṉ.
Published in S.I.I., Vol. XIX No. 133.

inscription.head:
No. 22.
(A.R. No. 234A of 1911.)
KUMBHAKŌṆAM, KUMBHAKONAM TALUK,
TANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the north and west walls of the central shrine, Nāgēśvara temple.

p.b: Year 5, Āni, [su] 5 Mūla, Friday: = 975 A.D. June 16.

inscription.p:
Records that the Mūlaparaḍai-perumakkaḷ of Tirukkuḍamukkil, a dēvadānam, in
Pāmbūr-nāḍu on the northern bank (of the river Kāvēri) received 20 kāśu from a
lady Madēvan Kaṇḍi, a servant maid of the Palace-establishment of (the queen)
Kaṟṟaḷi-Pirāṭṭiyār of Tañjāvūr in Tañjāvūr-kūṟṟam and sold a quarter vēli of land for
the maintenance of a person including his clothing who was appointed to bring a pot
of water from the (river) Kāvēri thrice a day for the sacred bath of the deity. The
ūr-kaṇakku wrote the sale-deed at the behest of mūlaparuḍaipperumakkaḷ.
Published in S.I.I., Vol. XIX No. 131.

inscription.head:
No. 23.
(A.R. No. 407 of 1903.)
TIRUVĀMATTŪR, VILLUPURAM TALUK,
SOUTH ARCOT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the north wall, central shrine, Abhirāmēśvara temple.

Records a gift of land after purchase by Nandipputtaṉ alias Śembiyaṉ Mūvēndavēḷāṉ,
a resident of Paṉaippākkam in Kīḻāṉmūr-nāḍu, a division of Tirumuṉaippāḍi for
burning one and a quarter perpetual lamp to the God of Tiruvāmāttūr, a dēvadānam
in Vāvalūr-nāḍu. The tiruvuṇṇāḻigai-sabhaiyār, received the land from the donor.
The donor purchased the land required from a lady Perumāṉaṅgaichchāṉi, the wife
of Āridaṉ Nandi Nārāyaṇaṉ of Tiruvāmāttūr. It is also recorded that the said land
had come to her possession as her maintenance and now she sold it through her
guardian (mudukaṇ) Atiyā Saṉmadi^1^ to the above donor.
Published in S.I.I., Vol. VIII No. 723.

inscription.head:
No. 24.
(A.R. No. 259 of 1923.)
KŌYIL-TĒVARĀYAṈPĒṬṬAI, PAPANASAM TALUK,
TANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the south wall, central shrine, Matsyapurīśvara temple.

This inscription records the grant of three pieces of land after purchase by an
individual Tiruvaḍigaḷ Aiyanaḍi of Śiruvēḷūr in Iḷaiyūr-nāḍu on the southern bank of
the river (Kāvēri) and endowed the same for various purposes to the god, Tiruchchelūr
Mahādēvar at Rājakēsari-chaturvēdimaṅgalam.

He purchased the first piece from several individuals of Maṉōramachchēri in
Rājakēsari-chaturvēdimaṅgalam and endowed the same for supplying an uri of oil
daily for burning a perpetual lamp and also for providing food offerings during the
early morning and midday services to the god.

The second piece of land had been purchased during the eighth regnal year of
Rājakēsarivarmaṉ (Sundarachōḷa ?) for maintaining a garden for the temple.

The third plot of land had been purchased from Sāvandi Nārāyaṇaṉ and gifted
for the sake of providing flowers in exchange for a plot of four mā which was
probably already in the enjoyment of the temple for the same purpose.
Published in S.I.I., Vol. XIX No. 139.
