inscription.head:
No. 221.
(A.R. No. 300 of 1993-94.)
VRIDDHĀCHALAM, VRIDDHACHALAM TALUK, SOUTH ARCOT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the south wall (maṇḍapa) at the left side, Vriddhagirīśvara temple.

Built in. Seems to refer to a benefaction for burning a perpetual lamp
probably by the daughter of Viḻupparaiyar, who was the wife (name lost).

inscription.head:
No. 222.
(A.R. No. 3 of 1906.)
KĀÑCHĪPURAM, KANCHIPURAM TALUK, CHINGLEPUT DISTRICT.
inscription.p:
On a stone built into the floor at the entrance into the Smāśānēśvara shrine of
Ekāmranātha temple.

The right hand portion of the inscription is lost. An undertaking given by
the kaḍavuḷ-sabhā (sabhā for temple management) of Tirumiyāṉapuram, for carrying
out some service for the God Brahmīśvaratt-āḻvār in Tirumiyāṉam of Kachchippēḍu
on receiving thirty kaḻañju of gold, to the queen Vīranārāṇi.
Published in S.I.I., Vol. XXII No. 3.

l.ta:
kaṭṭaḷai kallāl tuḷainiṟai muppatiṉ kaḻañcu
poṉ koṇ ••• ••• ••••

inscription.head:
No. 223.
(A.R. No. B of 2001.)
SŌMĀṆḌĀRKUḌI, VILLUPPURAM TALUK, SOUTH ARCOT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On a stone slab kept in the Īśvara temple.

Damaged. It seems to record a gift of gold by Aṇṇāvaḷḷitti Malli, wife of
Aḻisu Kāḍaiyār Kavira Sikhāmaṇiyār, for burning a lamp to the god Tiruchchōmīśvarar
of Pālūr in Pālūr-kūṟṟam, a sub-division of Milāḍu.

l.ta: rātittaval ko •• vicuran kuṭutta poṉ por ka •••••

inscription.head:
No. 224.
(A.R. No. 204 of 1925.)
TALACHCHAṄGĀḌU, MAYAVARAM TALUK, TANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p:
On a pillar set up on the north side of the central shrine in the
Śaṅkhāraṇyēśvara temple.

Records that Pirāntakaṉ Mādēvaḍigaḷ alias Sembiyan Mahādēvi, the mother of
Uttamachōḷa, donated a silver palanquin (veḷḷichchapparam) to the God Mahādēva in
the big temple at Talaichchaṅgāḍu.

l.note:
There is an inscription of three lines at the begining which reads
1. svastiśrī
2. kōrājakē[cari]
3. pamakku

inscription.head:
No. 225.
(A.R. No. 28 of 1907.)
TIRUVIŚALŪR, KUMBHAKONAM TALUK, TANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the north wall, central shrine, Śivayōganāthasvāmi temple.

Damaged. Seems to record that Sembiyaṉ Mahādēvi, the mother of
Parakēsarivarman alias Śrī-Gaṇḍarāditta Madhurāntakadēvar, donated several ornaments
to Tiruviśalūr Paramasvāmi, after having the sacred bath of the God performed.
Published in S.I.I., Vol. III No. 148.

edition.l:
svastiśrī [||*] koparakesari[pa]nmarāṉa śrī kaṇḍarātitta [śrī] maturā
[ ntakarai] t ti[ruvayi]ṟu vāytta uṭaiya ••• ••••• Read pirāṭṭiyār cempi

inscription.head:
No. 226.
(A.R. No. 219 of 1965.)
EŚAIYAṈŪR, WALAJAPET TALUK, NORTH ARCOT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: Tiers of the southern wall, Akhilāṇḍēśvara temple.

Damaged and incomplete. Seems to record an agreement by the ūrār of
E[śaya]ṉūr-nāḍu in Dāmar-kōṭṭam in respect of a grant of land made tax-free to
provide five drummers and food offerings for śrībali during the paḷḷieḻuchchi service
to the god.

inscription.head:
No. 227.
(A.R. No. 626 of 1909.)
KŌNĒRIRĀJAPURAM, TANJAVUR TALUK, TANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the south wall, central shrine, Umāmahēśvara temple.

This inscription is a label explaining an image. It states that it is that of
Śāttaṉ Guṇabhattaṉ alias Haracharaṇa-śēkharaṉ of Ālattūr, who had also been
honoured with the title, Rājakēsari-mūvēndavēḷār who built the temple in stone,
possibly under the direction of Uḍaiya-pirāṭṭiyār, mother of Madhurāntakadēva alias
Uttamachōḷa.
Published in S.I.I., Vol. III No. 147.

inscription.head:
No. 228.
(A.R. No. 131 of 1926.)
TIRUVIḶAKKUḌI, MAYURAM TALUK, TANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the south wall of the central shrine, Maṇavāḷēśvara temple.

Records the construction of quarter portion of the stone temple for the god of
Tiruvēḻvikkuḍi by the itinerant Vaḷañjiyar and Nānādēśiya-diśaiyāyittaiññūṟṟuvar
(the mercantile communities), and the completion of the same by Tiruvēḻvikkuḍi
Nambi alias Tiruvaiyāṟu Yōgiyār and his son Kaṟṟaḷippittar who was the clerk
(Śrīkaraṇam) of the village. The two persons were fondly described as the children
of the above mercantile bodies.
Published in S.I.I., Vol. XIX. No. 459.

edition.l:

inscription.head:
No. 229.
(A.R. No. 17 of 1920.)
TIRUMAḺAVĀḌI, UDAIYARPALAIYAM TALUK,
TIRUCHIRAPPALLI DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the west wall, central shrine, Vaidyanātha temple.

Beginning is damaged. It records a gift of 4 mā of land in two pieces after
purchase for thrity kaḻañju of gold, for providing mid-night service (in the temple)
by Pirāntakan Mādēvaḍigaḷ.
Published in S.I.I., Vol. XIX No. 167.

note.p:
An inscription attributed to Uttamachōḷa's period, is found engraved on the south wall mukhamaṇḍapa
of Jambuliggasvāmi temple at ŚirukaṉpUr, Perambalur Taluk and district. The text runs as follows:

3. n tirumaṇṭapam [||*]
ṣee ṭamiḻnaṭṭuk kalveṭṭukaḷ Vol. III (2010) P. 104. But the inscription is yet to be critically examined.

inscription.head:
No. 230.
(A.R. No. 577 of 1920.)
UḌAIYĀRGUḌI, CHIDAMBARAM TALUK, SOUTH ARCOT DISTRICT.
inscription.p:
On the west wall, central shrine in the Anantēśvara temple.
King: Rājakēsarivarman (Rājarāja I)

This inscription opens by stating that a letter (śrīmukham) was sent by the
emperor to the peruṅguṟi-pperumakkaḷ (members) of Śrī Vīranārayaṇach-
chaturvēdimaṅgalam (situated) on the northern bank (of the river Kāvēri). According
to that Sōman, his younger brothers, Ravidāsan alias Pañchavan brahmādhirājaṉ and
his younger brother Paramēśvaraṉ alias Irumuḍich-chōḷa brahmādhirājan, had been
found guilty of treason as they murdered Karikāla chōḷa, who took the head of
Pāṇḍya. As per the order, the properties (of the above two persons) their brother
Malaiyaṉūrāṉ, of their younger brothers and sons, of their wives and other kith and
kin were to be taken over by Brahmaśrīrājan of Kōṭṭaiyūr and Chandrasēkharabhaṭṭan
of Puḷḷamaṅgalam.

The inscription records further that the sabhā were to arrange in co-operation
with these two persons for the sale at current prices of all these lands and to remit
the proceeds to the treasury in accordance with the royal order. The order concludes
with the statement that it was written by Kurukkāḍi-Kīḻāṉ.

Accordingly two and odd vēli of land (as per the old measurement) and six
residential houses owned by Ravidāśa (the second traitor), his mother and his sons
were seized by the sabhā. These were purchased for 112 kaḻañju of gold from the
sabhā by Bharatan alias Vyāḷagajamallaṉ, the son of Nakkaṉ Aravaṇaiyāṉ of
Veṇṇaiyūr. He inturn endowed the same to the Tiruvanantēśvara-bhaṭṭārakar temple
at the above village in order to defray ten nāḻi of paddy daily and also a kāśu per
annum to a brāhmaṇa for supplying drinking water in the maṇḍapa, before the
temple, called Mūvāyirattu-aru-nūṟṟuvaṉ and also for the daily feeding of sixteen
brāhmaṇas, including five Śivayōgins, at the temple.
Published in Ep. Ind., Vol. XXI pp. 168-170.

inscription.head:
No. 231.
(A.R. No. 570 of 1904.)
TIRUVĀRŪR, NAGAPATTINAM TALUK, TANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the south wall, Achalēśvara shrine, Tyāgarājasvāmin temple.

This inscription belongs to the reign of Rājakēsarivarman evidently that of
Rājarāja I. It records a gift of two silver vessels in which offerings were to be made
to god Tiruvaraneṟi-Āḻvār by Uḍaiyapirāṭṭiyār Sembiyaṉ-Mahādēviyār for the merit
of Uttamachōḷa. She sent these vessels to the temple under the supervision of (her)
bodyguard Nīlaṉ Mādēvaṉ.
Published in S.I.I., Vol. XIII No. 1.

viyār

inscription.head:
No. 232.
(A.R. No. 57 of 1918.)
VRIDDHĀCHALAM, VRIDDHACHALAM TALUK, SOUTH ARCOT DISTRICT.
inscription.p:
On the north wall of the maṇḍapa in front of the central shrine,
Vriddhagirīśvara temple.

It records that a village Mūlakkuḍi, had been assigned as kuḍinīkka-dēvadānam
after demarcating the boundaries as dēvadāna by the king Uttamachōḷa and entrusted
to two persons Aruḷaṉ Siṅgaṉ and Guṇavaṉ Maṉimalan. Out of the land allotted to
them, during the reign of Rājarāja I (988 A.D.) seven mā of land in Vēppanchey
(possibly a block of land distinguished by the presence of Margosa trees in it) an
another one mā in Madhurāntaka-viḷāgam were entrusted as kāṇi along with a house
site to Pāradāyaṉ Kumaranārāyaṇa-bhaṭṭaṉ alias Tirumudukuṉṟa Irāśasiṅga Misuṇṇi
enjoining him to supply sandal paste and the incense powder for the daily use of the
deity and for the ingredients of the sacred bath on the days of Uttarāyaṇa and
Dakshiṇāyana days as well as on the days of Saṅkrānti every month to the God at
Tirumudukuṉṟam, a hamlet of Neṟkkuppai in Paruvūr-kūṟṟam in Iruṅgōḷappāḍi.
Published in S.I.I., Vol. XIII. No. 52.

inscription.head:
No. 233.
(A.R. No. 338 of 1927.)
TIRUPPUṞAMBIYAM, KUMBHAKONAM TALUK, TANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the south wall, central shrine, Sākshīśvara temple.

Records a gift of a silver pot weighing 143(3/4) kaḻañju and 2 mañjāḍi by
Uḍaiyapirāṭṭiyār, the mother of Śrī-Uttamachōḷa for the merit of her son to the god
at Tiruppuṟambiyam. It is further recorded that as per the weighing balance (tilāk-
kōl), called turaiyakkali, the gifted vessel weighed 13 kaḻañju and 1 palam and as per
the weighing stone of the ūr, it weighed 140 kaḻañju 18 mañjāḍi and 6 mā.

inscription.head:
No. 234.
(A.R. No. 444 of 1918.)
TIRUVEṆKĀḌU, SHIYALI TALUK, TANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the north wall, central shrine, Śvētāraṇyēśvara temple.
Rājarāja I.

It records in detail the stock of several gifts made to the temple at different
times. These comprised copper-vessels for the sacred bath of the deity presented in
the 4th year of Uttama-chōḷa by queen-mother Parāntakaṉ-Mādēviyār, a golden vessel
(koḷgai) set with gems presented by her in the 6th year of the same king, a golden
pot for pinnacle over the temple presented by her in the 3rd year of Rājakēsarivarman,
evidently Rājarāja I; another set of rich presents made in the same year by the
same lady, consisting of a golden image of Chandraśēkharadēva with a gold-plated
copper pedestal and silver aureola and ornaments set with gems, besides a silver plate
for offerings to the same deity; a silver pot and some gold ornaments by
Sōrabbaiyār Tribhuvanamādēviyār, the queen of Uttama-chōḷa presented in the same
year, and a gold flower by the same queen presented in that year in lieu of land
gift for the Śivaśrāddha of her husband; some gold ornaments made with money
presented to the temple by Parakēsarivarman (evidently Uttama-chōḷa himself) in his
10th and 11th years; a gold plate (paṭṭam) presented in the 4th year of Uttama-chōḷa
by Kōyiṟpiḷḷaiyār to the god, two lamp stands by Parāntakaṉ-Mādēvi and two by
Madhurāntaka (king); lastly 400 kāśu given by Parāntakaṉ-Mādēvi in the 2nd year
of (her husband) Gaṇḍarāditya alias Mummaḍi-chōḷadēva towards the saṅkrānti bath
of the deity every month and which was now in the 6th year of Rājakēsarivarman,
converted into a land endowment by the assembly of Nāṅgūr. At the end is
recorded another gift of 125 kaḻañju presented by the same queen for feeding a
brāhmaṇa in the temple for the merit of her servant (aḍiyāḷ) Ilaichchiyan Maḻapāḍi,
for which the assembly sold a land to her for endowment.
Published in S.I.I., Vol. XIII, No. 144.

inscription.head:
No. 235.
(A.R. No. 480 of 1925.)
SEMBIYANMĀDĒVI, NAGAPATTINAM TALUK, TANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p:
On the north base of the central shrine, Kailāsanāthasvāmi temple.
Rājarājakēsari (Rājarāja I)

This inscription records a directive to the chaturvēdibhaṭṭat-tānapperumakkaḷ of
Sembiyanmahādēvi-chaturvēdimaṅgalam, a brahmadēyam in Aḷa-nāḍu, on the southern
bank (of the river Kāvēri), founded by the queen Śrī-Parāntakaṉ-Mādēvaḍigaḷ alias
Śrī-Sembiyanmahādēviyār, the mother of Uttama-chōḷa asking them to feed themselves
in the temple śrī Kayilāsam-uḍaiya mahādēvar on the day of Tirukkēṭṭai in the
Chittirai month, the natal star of the above queen with the interest on 100 kāśu
endowed by Ubhaṭṭayaṉ Sōrabbai alias Tribhuvanamahādēvi, the queen of Uttama-
chōḷa.

inscription.head:
No. 236.
(A.R. No. 262 of 1923.)
KŌYIL-TĒVARĀYAṈPĒṬṬAI, PAPANASAM TALUK,
TANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the south wall, central shrine, Matsyapurīśvara temple.

This is an inscription of Rājarāja I and it records a gift of a silver plate and
a bronze stand to God Mahādēva in the temple at Tiruchchelūr by Sembiyaṉ
Mahādēviyār, the mother of Uttama-chōḷa for the merit of her son.

inscription.head:
No. 237.
(A.R. No. 263 of 1923.)
KŌYIL-TĒVARĀYAṈPĒṬṬAI, PAPANASAM TALUK,
TANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the south wall, central shrine, Matsyapurīśvara temple.

This is an inscription of Rājarāja I and it records a gift of gold pot to the god
Mahādēva of Tiruchchelūr by Sembiyan Mahādēviyār, the mother of Uttama chōḷa
for the merit of her son.

edition.l: viyār tanta poṟkkalacam 1-

table.c4:
Information from
T N S A Tamilnadu State Department of Archaeology.

part.p:
A few inscriptions, which were recently copied are inclūded in this volūme on
accoūnt of their importance.

An inscription (No. 1) of Rājakēsarivarmaṉ is foūnd engraved on the soūth base
of the Kailāsanātha temple at Uttiramērūr. This inscription has been assigned to the
Chōḷa rūler, Āditya I The Hindu, dated 20.11.08. p. 22.(871-890 A.D.) But the mention of the garden committee in
this inscription suggests a later date Personal commūnication from Dr. J.Sundaram. He is of the opinion that this inscription
mūst be assigned to a Rājakēsarivarman who rūled after Parāntaka I. He feels that this
record belongs to Sundarachōḷa (957-973 A.D.) who is also called Rājakēsarivarman.since sūch committee first appears dūring the
reign of his son and sūccessor, Parāntaka I (907-955 A.D.). Two celebrated
inscriptions of Uttiramērūr A S I A R. 1904-05, PP. 131 ff.dated in the said king's reign refer to the garden
committee for the first time while detailing the electoral reforms pertaining to the
mode of selection of the members who wished to serve in the varioūs committees
of the brāhmaṇical assembly, Uttiramērū-chatūrvēdimaggalam. However, the location
of the inscription in Uttiramērūr, the findspot of the inscription on the base of the
temple as well as the palaeography of the record tend to sūpport its assignment to
the period of Āditya I. It must be noted that the famous inscription Ep. Ind., Vol. VIII, p. 260.of this rūler
at the nearby hamlet, Tirūkkaḻukkūṉṟam recording the renewal of the earlier grants
of his predecessors in Pallava times, also reinforces the fact that Rājakēsarivarman of
the Uttiramērūr inscription is indeed Āditya Chōḷa. If this view is correct we may
ventūre to sūggest the existence of sūch committee(s) at Uttiramērūr even before the
period of Parāntaka I. Inscriptions of the Pallava rūler, Kampavarmaṉ, (846-878 A.D.) from Uttiramērūr records
the activities of tank committee. See S.I.I. Vol. VI, Nos. 294, 348 etc; It seems qūite
possible that garden committee may have also been in existence at Uttiramērūr dūring the
Pallava period. Only fūtūre discoveries and research will be able to provide confirmation
to this.

Tirūchchendūṟai near Tirūchirappalli has yielded two inscriptions (Nos. 2 and 3).
They are foūnd engraved on the basement (jagati) of the central shrine of the
Chandraśēkhara temple. The first of these inscriptions Recently copied in 2009. Appendix No. 2.is dated in the 23rd regnal
year of Parakēsarivarmaṉ. It records that Madhūrāntaka Irūkkūvēḷ alias Āditta
Bhūti made a gift of land to the god of the stone temple at Tirūchchendūṟai on the
occasion of the first feeding of his son, annaprāyam (anna prāsanam), Bhūti Parāntakaṉ.

The high regnal year of this rūler, the palaeography of this record and the
identity of the donor Āditta Bhūti alias Madhūrāntaka Irūkkūvēḷ, a feūdatory of
Parāntaka I, makes this record assignable to Parāntaka I, K.V.Subrahmanya Aiyer, Historical Sketches of Ancient Dekhan, Vol. II. pp. 209 ff.who assūmed the epithet,
Parakēsarivarmaṉ. The temple was constructed in stone by the Irūkkūvēḷ chief's
daughter, Naggai Bhūti Āditta Piḍāri, who made several grants of land for running
various services in the temple. S.I.I. Vol. VIII, Nos. 601, 608, 618, 619, 620, 621.Another inscription Recently copied in 2009. Appendix No. 3.which is found incised in
continuation of the above inscription, and in same characters refers to her building
of the temple, in stone and also to a grant of one hundred kaḻañjū of gold to reclaim
the land and endowed the same to the temple in order to provide food offerings to
the god, Tirūchchendūṟai-Perūmāṉaḍigaḷ.

Six inscriptions of the RāshṭrakūTa ruler, Kṛishṇa III have been discovered during
the last few years. See Appendix Nos. 5-10.Like other inscriptions in the Tamil coūntry, they record the
assignment of land for the maintenance of village tank, for beating drūm dūring the
temple ceremonies as well as recording the sabhā's varioūs activities.

Among these is one found in the ancient village of Poygainallūr in Arakonam
Talūk. Recently copied. See Appendix No. 10.Dated in his 29th regnal year of this rūler (968 A.D.), it records an
undertaking given by the sabhā of Poygainallūr to provide food offerings to the gods
of Āmmūtti-vishṇugṛiham and Tirūvagattīśvaram of the village, oūt of the land grant
made by an individual, Mādhava Bhūtikramavittaṉ.

This sort of endowment providing for both the Vishṇu and Śiva temples in the
same village reveals the catholicity and co-operation that prevailed among various
sects in this period, as well as the fact that the Śiva temple of the village, which is
today ruined and neglected, was once in a flourishing state.

At Sāḷuvagkūppam, near Mahābalipūram the effect of the excavations in 2004
was to reveal a previoūsly unknown stone structūre. This is said to be a
Subrahmaṇya temple, which jūdging from the available records Copied in 2005. Being included in the Annual Report.came into existence
dūring the time of the Pallava rūler, Nandivarman II (731-796 A.D.) an area which
the inscriptions refer to as Tirūviḻichchil. Two inscriptions of the Rāshṭrakūṭa ruler,
Kṛishṇa III Ibid. See Appendix Nos. 5 and 9.may be studied here. The first one dated in his 21st regnal year
(960 A.D.), engraved on a broken rectangular stone, refers to the gift for conducting
worship at the Mahādēva temple at Tirūviḻichil in Āmūr-kōṭṭam. The other one
(965 A.D.) is found engraved on a boulder and refers to the assignment of land to
god Subrahmaṇya at Tirūviḻichil. It cannot be ascertained precisely when this temple
fell into ruin, today it has been reduced to mere rubble, with stone slabs strewn here
and there.

inscription.head:
No. 1.
(A.R. No. B of 2008-09.)
UTTIRAMĒRŪR, KANCHIPURAM TALUK, CHINGLEPUT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the base, south wall, Kailāsanātha temple.

This incomplete inscription on the grounds of palaeography can be assigned to
the 9th century A.D. Hence it may belong to the reign of Āditya I.

It records an agreement made by the members of the garden committee with
Dēvandaidēvaṉai Diraṉ, (a resident) of Northern bazaar, who made a gift of lamp-
stand, to the god of the big temple of Mugappe Brahmīśvaram and after his
disappearance (expiry ?) (ivaṉ illaiyāṉa piṟagū) the above committee received from
his son, Ādittaṉ nine kaḻañjū of gold weighed by the stone (eḍar pūram) and
undertook the responsibility of measūring oūt an uḻakkū of oil daily (for būrning the
lamp).

inscription.head:
No. 2.
(A.R. No. B of 2008-09.)
TIRUCHCHENDUṞAI, TIRUCHIRAPPALLI TALUK,
TIRUCHIRAPPALLI DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the jagati (south), central shrine, Chandraśēkhara temple.

This incomplete inscription records that Madhurāntaka Irukkuvēḷ alias Āditta
Bhūti made a gift (dakshiṇai) of four vēli of land at Veṇbhūtaṅkuḍi in Uṟaiyūr-
kuṟṟam, as a tax free dēvadāna to the god of the stone temple, at Tiruchchenduṟai
in Īśānamaṅgalam, a brahmadēyam, for providing food offerings, (to the god) on the
occasion of the first-feeding (annaprāyam) of his son Bhūti Parāntakan. The
apportionment of the yield, from the land of four hundred kalam of paddy is
detailed.

inscription.head:
No. 3.
(A.R. No. B of 2008-09.)
TIRUCHCHENDUṞAI, TIRUCHIRAPPALLI TALUK,
TIRUCHIRAPPALLI DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the jagati (south), ardhamaṇḍapa, Chandraśēkhara temple.

This inscription is found engraved in continuation of the last inscription.
It seems to be connected with it. The style of writing of both the records is similar.
It records that the paruḍai (brāhmaṇical assembly) granted land to the god of the
stone temple, as dēvadāna and agreed to protect the land from taxes such as
echchōṟu, veṭṭi and iṟai. It also states that Naṅgai Bhūti Āditta Piḍāri, who had built
the stone temple at Tiruchchenduṟai, gave one hundred kaḻañju of gold to reclaim
this half vēli and 1/8 mā of land and converted it into a field, called Piṉṉāmaḻai-
vayakkal and agreed to provide food offerings to the god.

inscription.head:
No. 4.
(A.R. No. B of 2008-09.)
PŪLĀṄKUṞICHCHI, TIRUPPATTUR TALUK,
RAMANATHAPURAM DISTRICT.
inscription.p:
On a stone set-up in a field (situated) at the base of the hillock called
Madagumalai.

It records that Śūṟan Nāgai, who is residing at Konṟaiyūr in Ollaiyūr-kūṟṟam
made an image of Aiyyaṉ (Aiyyaṉār) and it (the image) was placed under the
protection (of the mercantile body) tigai āyiratt-aiññūṟṟuvar.
Published in Āvaṇam, issue No. 19. p. 62.

inscription.head:
No. 5.
(A.R. No. B of 2004-05.)
SĀḶUVAṄKUPPAM, KANCHIPURAM TALUK,
KANCHIPURAM DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On a broken slab in the dilapidated Subrahmaṇya temple.

Damaged. It records the gift of one hundred and eighty nāḻi of oil per year
to be supplied to the god, Mahādēva of Tiruviḻichchil in Āmūr-kōṭṭam, at the rate
of sixty nāḻi of oil during the months of Māśi, [Kārttigai] and Aippasi. Other details
are lost.

l.note: May be read as mācāttu vāṇikarkaḷom ?

inscription.head:
No. 6.
(A.R. No. B of 2003-04.)
SEMBARAMPĀKKAM, SAIDAPET TALUK, CHINGLEPUT DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On a stone lying in front of the Sakthīśvara temple.

p.b:
King: Kannaradēva, who took Kachchi and Tañjai
Year 23: 962 A.D.

inscription.p:
This records the gift of 3000 kuḻi of land for beating drum thrice during the
śrībali ceremony to the God of Saktīśvaram temple at Sembarambākkam in Puliyūr-
kōṭṭam by Nāgaipperumāṉ of Kuḷattūr, his brothers Niṟaikali and Sēndabhaṭṭaṉ. The
ūrār of Sembarambākkam agreed to free the land from taxes. The priests of the
temple, Sembūdikiḻava Ēḻuvāḍiyaṉ, his brother Kōmayaṉ and his brother Aṅgi,
received the land and undertook the responsibility of getting the ceremony performed
without fail. It is also stated that they agreed to remit a fine of one mañjāḍi (of
gold) on those days when they could not perform the ceremony.
Published in Āvaṇam, Vol. XIII p. 10-11.

ttu eṅkaḷ kūṟṟut talaiyaṭi nūṟu kuḻi-

inscription.head:
No. 7.
(A.R. No. B of 2006-07.)
IḌAICHERUVĀI, TITTAKKUDI TALUK, CUDDALORE DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On a stone slab erected in the field of an individual.

It records that uḍaiyāṉ Mūrtti Tāyaṉ of Vāyalūrppāḍi in Ugaḷūr-kūṟṟam
made a gift of 1/8 vēli of land after purchase from the ūrār of Porōsappāḍi
Iḍaichchiṟu Vāyilūr and endowed it as ēripaṭṭi (for the maintenance of tank).

inscription.head:
No. 8.
TIRUPPĀLAIVAṈAM, PONNERI TALUK, CHINGLEPUT DISTRICT.
inscription.p:
On a stone etched on the floor of the prākāra, central shrine (north),
Pālaivaṉanāthar temple.
Information from the Tamil Nadu State Department of Archaeology.

p.b:
King: Kannaradēva, who took Kachchi and Tañjai
Year 25: 964 A.D.

inscription.p:
Damaged and incomplete. Mentions the big assembly of Eyilūr Nilaipulikkuḷam
in Paiyyūr-kōṭṭam and also an individual Nārāṇa Kaṭṭiyaraiyar of Uṟuppuṭṭūr, an
āḷuṅgaṇattār.

inscription.head:
No. 9.
(A.R. No. B of 2004-05.)
SĀḶUVAṄKUPPAM, KANCHIPURAM TALUK, KANCHIPURAM
DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the rock (east) to the north of Tiger-cave.

p.b:
King: Kannaradēva, who took Kachchi and Tañjai
Year 26: 965 A.D.

inscription.p:
Damaged and incomplete. Seems to refer to the grant of land as tiruvuṇṇāḻigai-
puṟam to the god, Subrahmaṇya-bhaṭṭārar of Tiruviḻichchil in Āmūr-kōṭṭam.

inscription.head:
No. 10.
(A.R. No. B of 2008-09.)
POYGAINALLŪR, ARAKONAM TALUK, VELLORE DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On a stone slab (broken into two pieces) kept inside the Perumāḷ temple.

Damaged at the end. It records an undertaking given by the sabhā of Poygaiñallūr
in Dāmar-kōṭṭam to provide food offerings to the gods of Āmmutti-Vishṇu-griham
and Tiruvagattīśvaram of the village (Poygaiñallūr), from the yield, out of the land
grant of five hundred kuḻi of two adjacent plots (one for each to the above
mentioned gods), made by Mādhava Bhūtakramavittaṉ (a resident) of Ayiṟkāṭṭūrnallūr.
The sabhā agreed to pay a penalty of 1/8 poṉ daily to the members of the
committee (vāriyam) who were discharging duties of that particular year, if there was
any default (on their part) in the supply of food offerings.

inscription.head:
No. 11.
(A.R. No. B 113 of 1895.)
KĪLAPPAḺUVŪR, UDAIYARPALAIYAM TALUK,
TIRUCHIRAPPALLI DISTRICT.
inscription.p:
On the east wall of the maṇḍapa in front of the central shrine,
Vaṭamūlēśvara temple.

Records that Kuṇavaṉ Vaḍavāyil, a merchant, residing at Pāmbuṇi in Pāmbuṇi-
kkūṟṟam made a gift of a copper pot, weighing seventy (palam ?) to the god,
Tiruvālanduṟai-Mahādēva of Śiṟupaḻuvūr, a brahmadēyam in Kuṉṟa-kkūṟṟam. The
temple servants (dēvakanmigaḷ) received the gift and earmarked it to the god,
Gaṇapatiyār.
Published in S.I.I., Vol. V No. 674.
